coredata.dc:description: Lysozyme, a well-known bacteriolytic enzyme, exhibits a fascinating yet complex behavior when it comes to protein aggregation. Under certain conditions, this enzyme undergoes flexible transformation, transitioning from partially unfolded intermediate units of native conformers into complex cross-β-rich nano fibrillar amyloid architectures. Formation of such lysozyme amyloids has been implicated in a multitude of pathological and medical severities, like hepatic dysfunction, hepatomegaly, splenic rupture as well as spleen dysfunction, nephropathy, sicca syndrome, renal dysfunction, renal amyloidosis, and systemic amyloidosis. In this comprehensive review, we have attempted to provide in-depth insights into the aggregating behavior of lysozyme across a spectrum of variables, including concentrations, temperatures, pH levels, and mutations. Our objective is to elucidate the underlying mechanisms that govern lysozyme's aggregation process and to unravel the complex interplay between its structural attributes. Moreover, this work has critically examined the latest advancements in the field, focusing specifically on novel strategies and systems, that have been implemented to delay or inhibit the lysozyme amyloidogenesis. Apart from this, we have tried to explore and advance our fundamental understanding of the complex processes involved in lysozyme aggregation. This will help the research community to lay a robust foundation for screening, designing, and formulating targeted anti-amyloid therapeutics offering improved treatment modalities and interventions not only for lysozyme-linked amyloidopathy but for a wide range of amyloid-related disorders.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical representation of lysozyme mutations: Fig. 1. illustrates key mutations in the lysozyme protein, represented as distinct regions with varying colors. These mutations, marked on the lysozyme structure, include (I56T, F57I, W64R, D67H, T70N, and F57I/T7 or W112R/T70N). Each mutation site is highlighted and color-coded for clarity. Fig. 1 b represents the domain structure of Human Lysozyme PDB ID: 1REX highlighting the various mutations and secondary structure. Fig. 1 c represents the domain structure of Hen Egg White Lysozyme PDB ID: 193L highlighting the secondary structure.

Progressive aggregation of human lysozyme and amyloid fibril formation. This schematic diagram illustrates the stepwise aggregation of Human Lysozyme, resulting in the formation of amyloid fibrils. These fibrils have been associated with various medical conditions and are a consequence of six mutations in specific amino acid residues of Human Lysozyme. The aggregated fibrils are known to deposit in various organs, contributing to the severity of these medical complications.

Antibacterial activity of lysozyme through NAM and NAG cleavage. This graphical representation visually illustrates the antibacterial activity of lysozyme, showcasing its mechanism of action through the cleavage of NAM (N-acetylmuramic acid) and NAG (N-acetylglucosamine) in bacterial cell walls.

Graphical representation that illustrates the factors influencing lysozyme aggregation under various in vitro and in vivo conditions, such as unfavorable conditions lysozyme misfolds and subsequently aggregates into toxic structures.

Categorization of phytochemicals and their association with lysozyme aggregation inhibition: In this figure, the oval shaped symbols blue denote the classification of phytochemicals, yellow coloured oval shape represent the sub-classification of phytochemicals within the polyphenols category. Phytochemicals that have exhibited a correlation with inhibiting lysozyme aggregation are marked with green colour, while those in sub-classes lacking such correlation and that have to be explored are represented in grey colored oval shape.

Effect of resveratrol on amyloid fibril formation of wild-type Human Lysozyme (Hul/HL). A) Structure of the compound resveratrol. B) Effect of resveratrol on HL fibrillization, as measured by means of the ThT assay. HL (70 μm) was incubated with resveratrol (10–136 μM) at pH 7.4 for 1 h and later the pH was shifted to 2 and the samples were incubated at 60°C and 270 rpm. All curves represent mean:standard deviation (SD; N = 3). C) Lag time. D) Apparent rate constant (kapp) of fibrillization of HL, as evaluated in the absence and presence of increasing resveratrol concentrations (10–136 μM), obtained by fitting the ThT kinetics data to a sigmoidal model. Data represent mean:SD (N = 3). E) Normalized light scattering of species formed at the end of fibrillization in the HL control and in the presence of increasing resveratrol concentrations (0–136 μM), as obtained at 90°C and λ = 500 nm in a fluorimeter. F) Dynamic light scattering (DLS) curves showing the progression of species size during the fibrillization of HL control alone, and in the presence of increasing resveratrol concentrations. G) TEM images depicting fibrillization performed i) in HL control alone and the presence of ii) 50, iii) 110, and iv) 136 μM resveratrol. Scale bars: 200 nm (10,000 V). Copyright 2023 John Wiley and Sons.

Molecular Interactions between Lysozyme Isoforms and Phytochemicals (a) Interaction diagram depicting the binding interactions between Hen Egg White Lysozyme (PDB ID: 193L) and the screened phytochemicals. (b) Interaction diagram illustrating the binding interactions between Native Human Lysozyme (PDB ID: 1REX) and the screened phytochemicals. The highlighted regions indicate distinct binding sites and interaction characteristics. These interaction figures provide valuable insights into the binding mechanisms.

Molecular docking analysis of oleuropein interaction with lysozymes: Molecular docking reveals the interaction of (a) human native lysozyme (PDB ID: 1REX) with oleuropein, with 8 prominent hydrogen bonds and 3 π-alkyl interactions with alanine, valine, glutamic acid, tryptophan, and glycine, with a binding affinity of −9.17 kcal/mol. (b) Hen egg white lysozyme (PDB ID: 193l) with oleuropein, with 8 prominent hydrogen bonds and 3 π-alkyl interactions with asparagine, alanine, valine, glutamic acid, tryptophan, and glycine, respectively, with a binding affinity of −8.81 kcal/mol.

Pathway of lysozyme aggregation and influencing factors: schematic diagram illustrating the pathway of lysozyme aggregation and influencing factors variables such as temperature, pH, and salt concentration can initiate fibril formation, leading to the accumulation of higher-order structures and the development of toxic amyloids. Inhibitors, including Dendrimers (Top Left), Micelles (Top Right), Carbon Nanotubes (Bottom Left), and Quantum Dots (Bottom Right), have been identified as agents that not only prevent lysozyme aggregation but also facilitate the re-folding of Lysozyme into its native structure. This diagram highlights key elements in the regulation of lysozyme aggregation and the potential for intervention in the process.
Currently identified types of Lysozyme present in various species.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lysozyme, a well-known bacteriolytic enzyme, exhibits a fascinating yet complex behavior when it comes to protein aggregation. Under certain conditions, this enzyme undergoes flexible transformation, transitioning from partially unfolded intermediate units of native conformers into complex cross-β-rich nano fibrillar amyloid architectures. Formation of such lysozyme amyloids has been implicated in a multitude of pathological and medical severities, like hepatic dysfunction, hepatomegaly, splenic rupture as well as spleen dysfunction, nephropathy, sicca syndrome, renal dysfunction, renal amyloidosis, and systemic amyloidosis. In this comprehensive review, we have attempted to provide in-depth insights into the aggregating behavior of lysozyme across a spectrum of variables, including concentrations, temperatures, pH levels, and mutations. Our objective is to elucidate the underlying mechanisms that govern lysozyme's aggregation process and to unravel the complex interplay between its structural attributes. Moreover, this work has critically examined the latest advancements in the field, focusing specifically on novel strategies and systems, that have been implemented to delay or inhibit the lysozyme amyloidogenesis. Apart from this, we have tried to explore and advance our fundamental understanding of the complex processes involved in lysozyme aggregation. This will help the research community to lay a robust foundation for screening, designing, and formulating targeted anti-amyloid therapeutics offering improved treatment modalities and interventions not only for lysozyme-linked amyloidopathy but for a wide range of amyloid-related disorders.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid polymerization and self-assembly properties of molecular entities such as proteins and peptides result in the formation of complex supramolecular structures known as protein amyloids. However, such protein amyloids are often associated with devastating medical conditions. A broad spectrum of over 50 human diseases, including well-known conditions such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and type II diabetes, are directly linked to the aggregation of precursor peptides and proteins [1].

Recent research highlights the importance of initial protein misfolding and disrupted homeostasis in the clearance mechanism, triggering a series of co-aggregation and cross-seeding events within our system. These events contribute to various complications associated with amyloid formation [2–5]. Additionally, the coexistence of multiple amyloid-related complications in the same individual prompts us to evaluate the amyloid cascade hypothesis and its biological significance in the current context [6–10]. Furthermore, recent research findings indicate that certain single molecules, metabolites, and neuromodulators /precursors tend to undergo phase separation, forming higher-ordered oligomeric and seeding-competent fibrillar entities. These entities are known to influence the physicochemical properties of proteins and biomolecules in their vicinity [11–13]. Likewise, the myo-granule protein TDP-43, situated in the nucleus of muscle stem cells, performs a crucial function in muscle formation and regeneration. However, it has been observed that TDP-43 also exhibits a propensity to aggregate and undergo phase separation, giving rise to the creation of supramolecular heterologous architectures [14]. The belief is that the structural polymorphism of TDP-43 significantly contributes to the formation of such assemblies, consequently initiating a cascade of cytotoxic events [15]. Despite the negative impact of protein aggregation, it is sometimes beneficial to the human system. For example, blood proteins like transferrin and fibrinogen are essential for clot formation and wound healing [16].

Lysozyme is an exceptional globular protein, known to maintain the innate immune system by modulating transcriptional and immune pathways [17,18]. This protein is primarily found in neutrophil granules, saliva, tears, intestinal Paneth cells, blood serum, and human milk. Apart from this, it is also found in certain bacteria that are enclosed by a peptidoglycan cell wall, and in bacteriophages to facilitate infection and/or host cell lysis through virion release [19]. Lysozyme, a small yet important protein with 130 amino acid residues with a molecular weight of 14.3 kDa. The groundbreaking discovery of lysozyme's inhibitory and lytic properties by Fleming in 1922 laid the foundation for further advancements in understanding this remarkable enzyme [20]. The pioneering work of Fleming and the subsequent efforts of Phillips and his team have significantly advanced the understanding of lysozyme, shedding light on its inhibitory and catalytic properties [21]. These landmark studies have paved the way for further research and exploration of this essential enzyme and its role in various biological processes. The thermodynamic profile of Lysozyme includes several parameters such as enthalpy, entropy, and Gibbs energy, which dictate its multiple motif conformations during renaturation and denaturation phases at varying pH values [22]. Such complex native structural integrity is a testament to Anfinsen's Dogma. Despite its versatility and importance, lysozyme tends to aggregate under certain conditions such as temperature, pH, salt concentration, protein concentration, light irradiation, ionic strength, chaperone action, mutations, and macromolecular crowding [23–25]. Shockingly, even lysozyme aggregates in our physiological milieu and is linked to systemic amyloidosis and are implicated in clinical features of visceral dysfunction and isolated nephropathy [26].

The formation of lysozyme aggregates stems from the transformation of their native α-helical and randomly coiled secondary structures into a distinct cross-β-rich fibrillar architecture, commonly referred to as amyloids. A notable study conducted by Radford and colleagues shed light on the crucial role of partially folded intermediates in the process of amyloid formation. This research elucidated the contributing factors behind the aggregation, instability, and unfolding of different variants of human lysozyme [27]. By exploring these mechanisms, scientists gain deeper insights into the complex processes underlying protein aggregation and its implications for various diseases and pathological conditions. It is understood that several variants of lysozyme have been identified and linked to various disease complications. Notably, the Asp67His variant has been found to undergo significant conformational changes compared to the wild-type lysozyme. This variant occurs due to the point mutation in the lysozyme by the substitution of highly conserved residue namely aspartic acid for histidine at the 67th position. These changes are primarily attributed to the destruction of hydrogen bonds that play a crucial role in stabilizing the β-domain of the protein. As a result, the structural stability of the Asp67His variant is compromised. On the other hand, the Ile56Thr variant shows no significant structural changes when compared to the native lysozyme. This suggests that this variant maintains a similar conformation to the wild-type protein, with no major alterations in its structure. Importantly, despite the structural changes observed in the variant forms, they do not engage in pathological aggregation. This can be linked to the higher stability exhibited by the wild-type lysozyme. Moreover, thermal stabilization does not play a significant role in protecting the hydrogen bonds, which are essential for maintaining the structural stability of the protein's interior or core. The different lysozyme variants exhibit distinctive characteristics, including an increased number of β-sheet domains, enhanced β-sheet conformers, and exposed hydrophobic regions. Such features contribute to the altered properties of these forms compared to the wild-type lysozyme [27].

Lysozyme amyloidosis, also known as hereditary non-neuropathic systemic amyloidosis (HNA), is associated with mutations in the lysozyme (LYZ) gene. It has been established that three distinct mutations p.Gly72Arg (G72R), p.Ala25Thr (A25T) p.Trp64Arg (W64R) in the lysozyme gene can lead to hereditary non-neuropathic renal amyloidosis. These findings were derived from a study involving three patients from an Italian-Canadian family, as documented by Valleix et al. in 2002 and further supported by the investigations of Yazaki, Farrell, and Benson in 2003. [28,29]. Reports suggest that the W64R variant exhibits similar biophysical and structural properties much alike to the other mutant I56T variant [30]. Recent studies have continued to strengthen the association between the accumulation of lysozyme, and its link to peripheral and familial amyloid polyneuropathies. This condition is characterized by notable symptoms such as severe proteinuria, nephrotic syndrome, and an increased risk of renal failure. [31–33].

Though, the versatile protein with its heterogeneous properties, is a fascinating subject of study in the field of immunology, biophysics, and molecular biology. Its complex native structure, thermodynamic profile, and propensity toward aggregation and fibril formation can have both positive and negative implications in various aspects of medical and industrial domains. Lysozyme aggregation has been a major goal for researchers worldwide, as this phenomenon can lead to various pathological conditions. By investigating the mechanisms of fibrillogenesis inhibition, researchers have uncovered several promising strategies for preventing lysozyme aggregation. Over the years, there has been a surge of research focused on developing effective inhibitors ranging from short peptides, nanoparticles, phytochemicals, etc., to prevent the aggregation of lysozyme. This comprehensive review digs deep into the array of methodologies presently accessible for countering lysozyme aggregation. It also provides an extensive array of knowledge that comprehends a wide-ranging exploration of inhibitors and nano-formulations, elucidating their underlying mechanisms in fortifying the lysozyme's structural integrity and its effect on amyloid oligomerization, spontaneous aggregation, and disassembly. Examining these phenomena in labyrinthine approaches, also offers an all-encompassing synopsis of the existing research landscape, while simultaneously outlining prospective avenues for the development of efficacious theragnostic strategies to combat lysozyme aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lysozyme: discovery, prevalence, molecular structure and variants
ce:section.ce:para: Lysozyme was originally discovered by Alexander Fleming when he identified it for the first time from nasopharyngeal mucus. Fascinatingly, Fleming also observed that lysozyme exhibited a remarkable bactericidal effect on Micrococcus lysodeikticus, by hydrolyzing the β-1,4 glycosidic bonds between the N-acetylmuramic acid and N-acetylglucosamine found in the cell wall of bacteria [34].

This enzyme was subsequently found to be present in various bodily secretions such as saliva, tears, sputum, nasal mucus, and even in blood serum. This widespread distribution further emphasized the potential significance of lysozyme in the body's defense against bacterial infections and sparked further research into its antibacterial properties. [20] Lysozymes exhibit notable diversity and can be categorized into three prominent groups: chicken-type (c-type), goose-type (g-type), and invertebrate-type (i-type) (Table 1 ). Each of these groups possesses distinct structural and functional characteristics. In addition to these prominent types, several other lysozyme variants have been identified such as phage-type, bacterial-type, and plant-type lysozymes, each exhibiting unique characteristics and functions(Table 1) [35,36]. Phage-type lysozymes are found in bacteriophages and serve as essential components in their life cycles. Bacterial-type lysozymes are present in certain bacteria which plays a vital role in their defense mechanisms. Lastly, plant-type lysozymes have also been identified in Carica papaya L, Brassica oleracea, and Hevea brasiliensis. The recognition of these different types of lysozymes underscores the remarkable adaptability and evolutionary significance of this enzyme highlighting the fascinating presence of this enzyme across various biological kingdoms. [35,36] (See Table 1)

C-type lysozymes possess a primary structure composed of a single peptide chain consisting of 129 amino acid residues, resulting in a molecular weight of approximately 14.3 kDa. This peptide chain is noteworthy for containing four intact disulfide bonds, which contribute to the protein's structural stability. These disulfide bonds are formed between specific cysteine residues, namely 6Cys–127Cys, 30Cys–115Cys, 64Cys–80Cys, and 76Cys–94Cys. Additionally, c-type lysozymes are characterized by the presence of six tryptophan (Trp) residues, three tyrosine (Tyr) residues, and three phenylalanine (Phe) residues. These aromatic amino acids play important roles in the protein's structure, function, and interaction with its target molecules. The specific arrangement and distribution of these amino acid residues contribute to the unique properties and enzymatic activity of c-type lysozymes [36]. In addition to the previously mentioned amino acid residues, it is worth noting that within lysozyme, Trp-62 and Trp-108 are considered the most critical and significant fluorophores. [37].

The structural characteristics of Embden goose lysozyme, which belongs to the g-type class of lysozymes, are remarkably similar to both hen egg-white lysozyme and phage lysozyme, despite lacking any significant sequence similarity to either of them. Intriguingly, specific regions of goose lysozyme exhibit motifs that closely resemble those of hen lysozyme, while other regions are unique to the phage enzyme [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the molecular structure of Hen Egg White Lysozyme
ce:section.ce:para: Hen Egg White Lysozyme (HEWL) stands as a revolutionary enzyme in the realm of X-ray crystallography, being the first to undergo detailed analysis of its three-dimensional structure [40]. Comprising 129 amino acids, this enzyme adopts a compact, globular conformation, manifesting an ellipsoidal shape with dimensions of 4.5 × 3.0 × 3.0 nm. Remarkably, it exhibits a conspicuous wide cleft on one side, which gracefully divides into two distinct domains. The first domain, encompassing residues 1–39 and 85–129, predominantly represents α helices, lending structural elegance to this portion. Conversely, the second domain (residues 40–84) projects an exquisite arrangement of parallel β sheets, contributing to its structural integrity (Fig. 1c) and Table 2. The harmonious orchestration of these domains enhances HEWL's enzymatic functionality and imparts finesse to its catalytic capability. By investigating the complexities of HEWL's lobe architecture, we gain captivating insights into its molecular structure underlying its remarkable enzymatic activity.

The Hen Egg White Lysozyme (HEWL) exhibits a structurally complex arrangement comprising various secondary structural elements. Notably, three α-helices are discernible on the protein surface, namely helix A (residues 4–15), helix C (residues 88–99), and helix D (residues 108–115). These helices are partially exposed to the solvent environment, rendering them accessible for interactions. On the other hand, α-helix B (residues 24–36) remains entirely buried within the protein's core, shielded from the solvent. In addition to the α-helices, the protein structure contains a 310 helix, which is partially exposed to the solvent at residues 119–124 and 104–107. The second lobe of HEWL (residues 40–84) exhibits a fascinating arrangement, encompassing a three-stranded antiparallel β-pleated sheet (residues 42–60), a small β sheet (residues 1–2 and 39–40), and a single-turn 310 helix (residues 79–84). This region is connected by a lengthy stretch of coiled-loop residues (residues 61–78), forming a vital link between the structural elements. The cleft region of the protein, which plays a significant functional role, is bordered by specific residues, including β-sheet residues 43, 44, 46, 52, and 56–59, helix B residue 35, the loop connecting helices C and D (residues 98 and 101–103), helix D residues 107–110 and 112, as well as residues 62, 63, and 73 (Fig. 1c) (See Table 2). The structural stability of HEWL is further reinforced by the presence of cysteine bridges between residues 6 and 127, 30 and 115, 64 and 80, and 76 and 94. These covalent bonds contribute to the overall rigidity and compactness of the protein, conferring a high level of stability and structural integrity [41]. These finicky secondary structural elements and cysteine bridges impart a remarkable level of order and sophistication to the architecture of Hen Egg White Lysozyme.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mutations and structural survey of human lysozyme and other types
ce:section.ce:para: Lysozyme is also present in humans, and its expression is governed by a gene located on chromosome 12. The gene is organized into four exons and three introns [56]. The enzyme exhibits a stretched conformation of muramic acid's sugar, facilitated by the amino residue's glutamic acid at position 35 and aspartic acid at position 52, which hydrolyze the glycosidic linkage. Lysozyme is primarily found in hematopoietic cells, including granulocytes, macrophages, and monocytes [57]. In the plasma, the normal concentration of lysozyme ranges between 4 and 13 mg/L. The body produces at least 500 mg of lysozyme per day, and approximately 75% of lysozyme is excreted through the kidneys within one hour [58]. The change in amino acid residue resulted in 6 mutant variants so far (Ile56 Thr, Phy57Ile, Trp64Arg, Asp67His, The70Asn, and Phe57I/Thr7 or Trp112Arg/Thr70Ala) (Fig. 1b). These mutants are linked with systemic amyloidosis(Fig. 2 ) except for Thr70Asn, [28,29,59], Thr70Asn is not amyloidogenic and is relatively prevalent in the normal British population [60]. The W64R mutation, constituting the third amyloidogenic alteration in lysozyme, was identified through genetic analysis and confirmed by the presence of amyloid deposits recognized by an anti-lysozyme antibody. In contrast to the Ile56Thr and Asp67His mutations, the Trp64Arg variant has not been isolated from ex-vivo fibrils within patients, leaving uncertainties regarding its fibril composition. Furthermore, unlike the well-characterized wild-type protein, the Trp64Arg protein is not detected in patient urine or plasma. Nonetheless, the W64R variant maintains the characteristic lysozyme fold with intact disulfide bonds. Upon exposure to protease, both the Trp64Arg and Ile56Thr proteins degrade within a span of 24 h, unlike the structurally intact wild-type protein. This observation suggests the presence of transiently unfolded states in the amyloidogenic variants, thereby exposing cleavage sites susceptible to protease activity. Notably, the W64R variant demonstrates a more rapid aggregation process compared to Ile56Thr, possibly attributed to an increased occurrence of intermediate species at a temperature of 60 °C under invitro conditions [30]. So based on such point mutations this lysozyme molecule is linked with various disease pathologies such as hepatic dysfunction, hepatomegaly, splenic rupture as well as spleen dysfunction, nephropathy, sicca syndrome, renal dysfunction, renal amyloidosis, and systemic amyloidosis as mentioned in Fig. 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Is there any structural similarity between Hen Egg White and Human Lysozyme?
ce:section.ce:para: The Hen Egg White Lysozyme and Human Lysozyme exhibit a notable amino acid sequence similarity of 76.9% with an identity of 60%. Despite this similarity, their structural compositions demonstrate some dissimilarities due to differences in their amino acid sequences. Particularly, within the 129-residue sequence, there are 51 amino acid variances between the two lysozymes. One specific distinction is the presence of an additional amino acid insertion between positions 47 and 48 in the sequence of hen lysozyme [61]. Human lysozyme, consisting of 130 amino acid residues, is organized into two distinct domains: the α-domain (residues 1–38 and 86–130) and the β-domain (residues 39–85). The α-domain encompasses four helices (Helix A: 5–14, Helix B: 25–35, Helix C: 90–100, and Helix D: 110–115) and three 310-helices (residues 81–85, 105–108, and 122–124). The β-domain comprises three strands forming a β-sheet, with residues 43–46, 51–54, and 59–60 participating in its formation. Crucial for structural stability, four intramolecular disulfide bonds are present, connecting cystine 6 and 128, cystine 30 and 116, cystine 65 and 81, and cystine 77 and 95 [62]. Of significant interest, certain regions within the human lysozyme sequence have been identified as amyloidogenic, particularly residues 25–33, 55–62, and 107–112 [47,55]. Understanding the amyloidogenic properties of these regions is vital for unraveling the mechanisms underlying protein misfolding and aggregation, which are associated with various neurodegenerative diseases.

Human lysozyme and HEWL (hen egg white lysozyme) have been extensively studied and utilized as reference proteins for investigating protein structure and function. These proteins have been instrumental in research on protein stability, the process of protein folding, and other related areas over the past three decades [27,42–46,48–53,63–73]. The structural characterization and identification of amyloidogenic regions in human lysozyme offer valuable insights into its molecular behavior and potential implications in disease pathology. Such knowledge aids in advancing our understanding of protein folding, misfolding, and amyloidogenesis, with potential implications in the development of therapeutic strategies for amyloid-linked diseases. While both lysozymes share functional characteristics and play essential roles, their structural divergence highlights the complexity and diversity in protein evolution. Understanding these differences is crucial for comprehending the unique properties and functions of these lysozyme variants in various biological contexts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The vital significance and biological functions of lysozyme
ce:section.ce:para: Lysozyme is widely acknowledged for its impressive antibacterial properties and has been extensively studied for its catalytic mechanism as a glycosidase. It plays a crucial role in breaking down the peptidoglycan component of bacterial cell walls by cleaving β-1, 4 glycosidic linkages between N-acetyl muramic acid and N-acetyl glucosamine. (Fig. 3 ) This catalytic action heavily relies on two active residues, aspartate-52 and glutamate-35, which effectively target the glycosidic bond and hydrolyze it. Remarkably, lysozyme's bactericidal effects extend beyond mere bacterial lysis, as it exhibits a broader spectrum of activity that includes Gram-negative bacteria. Studies have even suggested that its bactericidal activity, particularly against Gram-positive species, might involve mechanisms beyond the enzymatic dissolution of the bacterial membrane. It was observed that though lysozyme looses its enzymatic activity after denaturaton, it can still retain its bactericidal properties, indicating the involvement of alternative mechanisms [74]. One proposed mechanism suggests that lysozyme creates ordered ion channels by perforating the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane. This process leads to the release of cellular constituents, resulting in bacterial death. This mechanism contributes significantly to lysozyme's potent antibacterial efficacy [75,76]. It is understood that the heat-denatured Hen Egg White lysozyme (HEWL) has been found to exhibit an increased bactericidal effect against Gram-negative bacteria. This enhanced activity is attributed to the dimeric form of HEWL inserted into the bacterial membrane, causing disruption [77]. Additionally, synthetic helix-loop-helix (HLH) peptides located at the top lip of the active site cleft of both HEWL (residues 87–114) and human lysozyme (residues 87–115) have demonstrated significant antibacterial properties, including effective membrane permeabilization [78].

Further studies have identified specific lysozyme residues with antibacterial effects against Gram-negative bacteria like E. coli. For example, residues 15–21 and 98–108 [79], as well as residues 98–112 [80], have been shown to exert antibacterial activity. Usually, the attachment of lysozyme to phospholipid vesicles is driven by electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, providing insights into the mechanisms of lysozyme-membrane interactions [81]. A comprehensive model proposed by Giuliani, Pirri, and Nicoletto (2007) sheds light on the cell penetration activity of lysozyme and its membrane-disrupting effects. Cationic antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) with a higher affinity for lipopolysaccharide (LPS) may replace divalent cations and bind to LPS. This leads to the disruption of salt bridges between phosphate groups and divalent cations, facilitating the permeation of peptides through the bacterial membrane.

Apart from its actions on the cytoplasmic membrane, lysozyme peptides can accumulate and induce significant disruptions and disorganizations of the lipid membrane. This phenomenon results in the loss of membrane potential and additional membrane dysfunction, including the inhibition of ATP production and proton motive force, ultimately leading to cell death [82]. The absence of lysozyme genes M and P has been associated with increased susceptibility to respiratory tract infections, particularly those caused by Gram-positive bacteria. Interestingly, in mice, increased lysozyme levels demonstrated therapeutic effects against K. pneumoniae infection, suggesting that externally administered lysozyme might serve as an effective adjuvant in the treatment of K. pneumonia-induced pneumonia [83]. A study employing a cellulose and carboxymethyl cellulose matrix for food packaging found that lysozyme was most successful in inhibiting the development of E. coli when combined with lactoferrin [84].

Furthermore, serum lysozyme levels have been found to correlate with the total body mass of granulomas, indicating that lysozyme may serve as a potential biomarker in sarcoidosis [85]. The hydrolysate produced by lysozyme activity has been shown to enhance the secretion of Immunoglobulin A (IgA), activate macrophages, and rapidly remove bacterial pathogens [86,87].

Lysozyme exerts its influence not only on the cytoplasmic membrane but also on lipid membranes, where peptides accumulate and trigger significant disruptions and disorganizations. This, in turn, leads to the loss of membrane potential and additional dysfunction, including the inhibition of ATP production and proton motive force, culminating in cell death [82].

Lysozyme was also observed to possess anti-tumor properties. In the context of lymphoproliferative tumors, lysozyme interacts with the production of IL-2, an interleukin that subsequently enhances the production of T-regulatory lymphocytes [88]. Additionally, lysozyme can trigger the release of interferon by releasing polyribopyrimidine acid [89]. Its anti-tumor action is multifaceted, involving the direct activation of immune responses and the indirect strengthening of host immunity. Notably, a study conducted by Feng revealed that lysozyme facilitates the invasion and migration of A549 cells through the stimulation of Rho family proteins in associated cytoskeleton signaling pathways [90]. This study suggests that lysozyme may serve as a potential protein marker in the development and progression of lung cancer and holds significant implications for diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in this condition.

The elevated lysozyme levels in Barrett's oesophageal columnar epithelial cells bear insights into the mechanisms underlying Barrett's esophagus, a condition characterized by abnormal changes in the lower oesophageal lining. This condition is strongly associated with severe and longstanding gastroesophageal reflux disease. The presence of lysozyme protein in the formation of Barrett's esophagus emphasizes its potential as a promising biomarker, providing essential information for a more comprehensive understanding and improved management of the disease [91]. These findings underscore the significance of lysozyme in the pathogenesis of Barrett's esophagus, highlighting its potential role as a tool for facilitating early detection and guiding therapeutic interventions.

A meticulous examination comparing individuals with or without coronary heart disease has revealed a compelling correlation, where heightened lysozyme levels were associated with an augmented susceptibility to developing hypertension [92]. This specific connection accentuates the interplay between lysozyme and the development of hypertension, warranting further investigation to unveil the underlying mechanisms. Evidently, a notable revelation has been established, attributing significance of the invasion of monocytes and macrophages that secrete lysozyme in the context of ATII-(Angiotensin II) induced vascular dysfunction and arterial hypertension [93]. Apart from this lysozyme also has an inherent cross-seeding potential ie., once it aggregates it can trap the proteins present in its vicinity leading to loss of native function and additional toxic gain of function [2,94,95]. This insightful finding accentuates the pivotal role of lysozyme in vascular health and impels further exploration into its involvement in the regulatory pathways governing hypertension. The myriad of diverse findings showcased herein highlights the versatile and multifunctional nature of lysozyme, illuminating its potential as a promising avenue for therapeutic applications across a spectrum of disease conditions. Its multifaceted roles, ranging from antibacterial properties to involvement in cancer, cardiovascular health, and immune regulation, present an intriguing array of possibilities for harnessing its therapeutic potential. As we delve deeper into the molecular mechanisms and pathways involving lysozyme, the prospects for novel treatment modalities and interventions emerge, offering renewed hope in the quest to combat devastating diseases. The comprehensive understanding of lysozyme's multifunctionality not only enriches scientific knowledge but also paves the way for groundbreaking advancements in medical research and patient care.

In vitro, research on Lysozyme aggregation involves a diverse range of conditions and assays to study the behavior of protein aggregation. One of the prominent aspects of this aggregation process is the formation of amyloid structures when lysozyme's partially folded intermediates are highly populated. Consequently, most studies on lysozyme aggregation are conducted below its transition temperature. The transition temperature of lysozyme is a critical parameter as it marks the point at which the protein unfolds and loses its biological activity. Typically falling between 69 °C and 71.9 °C, the transition temperature can vary depending on the pH of the solution and the presence of other molecules [2]. Maintaining lysozyme below its transition temperature is crucial for studying its stability and behavior. If the temperature exceeds this threshold, the protein unfolds, leading to the loss of its biological activity. Various factors influence the transition of lysozyme, including acidic pH, the presence of denaturants, and incubation near the transition temperature Fig. 4 .

The in vitro conditions used for lysozyme aggregation include elevated temperatures from 37 °C,70 °C, and 74 °C, rapid heating and cooling, and incubation at ∼70 °C. Additionally, acidic pH levels of 2.0 and 4.0, as well as alkaline pH of 7.4, are explored. Interestingly, all these conditions resulted in the formation of fibrils. Among these conditions, the most rapid fibril production occurred at a pH of 2.0 with incubation at 65 °C. Furthermore, when lysozyme was treated with Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) at pH levels below 11.0, it induced aggregation due to an increase in its net positive charge in response to decreasing pH, reaching its maximum at pH 1.0. This change in the net charge facilitated interactions between SDS and the positively charged lysozyme, leading to oligomerization and the subsequent formation of amyloid fibrils [96].

In addition to factors like temperature, pH, and the presence of detergents like SDS, lysozyme misfolding and aggregation can also occur in the presence of 90% ethanol. A study conducted by Goda, and colleagues observed the formation of amyloid protofilaments and fibrils in Hen Egg White Lysozyme (HEWL) when exposed to ethanol solution. According to their findings, HEWL experienced an increase in helical and sheet content in the presence of ethanol, followed by a subsequent loss of helices and tertiary structure. This structural transition led to the assembly of β-sheets into amyloid protofilaments, which are the early building blocks of amyloid fibrils. [97]. Apart from ethanol at higher concentrations of Guanidine Hydrochloride (5–6 M), the HEWL protein undergoes denaturation, and fibrils may be broken during their production. In a study by Vernaglia and colleagues (2004), when NaCl and Na2SO4 were utilized to induce aggregation, they led to the formation of crystals. Interestingly, Na2SO4 formed approximately three times as many aggregates as NaCl, indicating a stronger attraction between Na2SO4 and the protein [98]. It is believed that the ionic nature of the solution shields the repulsive positively charged lysozyme molecules, enabling favorable interactions that promote aggregation [99].

To summarise the in vitro aggregation of Lysozyme various aggregation methods leading to the formation of amyloid structures are mentioned as follows:
(i)
At elevated temperatures ranging from 55 to 90 °C and under acidic conditions with a pH range of 1.6 to 4, lysozyme can aggregate. It also forms amyloids at alkaline pH.
(ii)
Exposure to 80–90% ethanol at temperatures around 20–25 °C triggers the aggregation.
(iii)
The presence of trifluoroethanol at 30% v/v concentration induces aggregation at either 37 or 65 °C while maintaining a neutral pH of 7.4.
(iv)
Treating lysozyme with 2-5 M guanidine hydrochloride at 50 °C and pH 6.3 also results in aggregation.
(v)
A two-step process involves fully reducing disulfide bonds first, followed by treatment at 25 °C and pH 4–5 with 90% ethanol, leading to aggregation.
(vi)
Under conditions of shear or turbulent flow, lysozyme aggregates at 57 °C at pH 2.
(vii)
Lastly, exposture to sonication steps can also facilitate lysozyme aggregation [100].
In the case of Human Lysozyme, several reports suggest the varied aggregating phenomena exhibited by the protein as pointed out below.

Analogously, Human Lysozyme aggregates under decompressing pressure, demonstrated by incubating the samples at 57 °C, pH 7.4, and upon releasing the compressed pressure from 3.5 kbar to 1 atm creating a decompression environment causing amyloid formation [101]. The presence of guanidium chloride expedites the aggregation process, furthermore, the T70N, I56T, and D67H mutant variants of human lysozyme unfold and aggregate 3, 30, and 160 times faster respectively, as compared to the wild-type human lysozyme in guanidium chloride [102]. In addition, pH 3, 60 °C [103] and 0.1 M K2HPO4 at pH 8.5, 55 °C for 45 mins [104] are also recognized as aggregation-inducing conditions.These diverse aggregation methods highlight the complex behavior of lysozyme and its propensity to form amyloid structures under various environmental conditions, providing valuable insights into its potential roles in biological processes and disease-related mechanisms.

To assess the aggregation kinetics, various techniques are employed, with one of the most common methods using the spectroscopic signal from Thioflavin-T(ThT). The binding of thioflavin T to fibrillar structures results in a significant increase in excitation and a considerable rise in fluorescence emission at 482 nm, indicating the presence of β-rich aggregates. In solution, the low energy threshold allows the benzylamine and benzyl thiole rings of ThT to freely rotate around their common CC bond, leading to minimal fluorescence emission. However, when ThT binds to the cross β-strands of the amyloids, its rotation becomes locked, preserving the excited state and causing high fluorescence emission [105]. For a more comprehensive understanding of the aggregation process, researchers utilize multiple spectroscopic techniques, including the use of thioflavin T, congo red and other fluorescent dyes, amyloid peptide-AIE conjugate of GNNQQNY-4-(1,2,2-Triphenylvinyl)benzoic acid (G7-TBA) [106], (bis(triphenylphosphonium) tetraphenyl ethene (TPE-TPP) [107] and another aggregation-induced emission (AIE) molecules [108]. Various biophysical techniques like Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy, size exclusion chromatography (SEC), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were also used. These techniques provide valuable information on the aggregation pattern, changes in secondary structure, the size distribution of aggregates in a solution, and the structure and shape of the aggregates, offering quantitative detail and higher resolution compared to TEM [109].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Understanding the role of various inhibitors in influencing lysozyme aggregation
Interplay of phytochemicals in lysozyme amyloid aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: Phytochemicals are naturally occurring compounds found in plants that have various beneficial effects on human health. These bioactive compounds are not considered essential nutrients but are believed to play a significant role in promoting health and preventing chronic diseases. Phytochemicals are responsible for the vibrant colors, flavors, and aromas of fruits, vegetables, herbs, and other plant-based foods. There are numerous classes of phytochemicals, each with unique properties and potential health benefits. Based on their structural characteristics, phytochemicals can be divided into several classes, the most well-known of which are polyphenols, which are distinguished by many hydroxylated aromatic rings. Polyphenols: These are diverse group of compounds found in plant-based foods, including flavonoids (such as quercetin, catechins, and anthocyanins) and phenolic acids (such as caffeic acid and ferulic acid). Polyphenols are known for their antioxidant properties, which help neutralize harmful free radicals in the body and protect cells from oxidative damage. Carotenoids: These are pigments responsible for the vibrant colors of fruits and vegetables, such as beta-carotene in carrots and lycopene in tomatoes. Carotenoids have antioxidant effects and are also known for their role in promoting eye health. Alkaloids: These nitrogen-containing compounds are found in various plants, including caffeine found in coffee, theobromine present in cocoa, and nicotine content in tobacco. Some alkaloids have stimulant effects, while others may have analgesic or anti-inflammatory properties. Sulfides and Organosulfur compounds: Found in garlic, onions, and cruciferous vegetables like broccoli and cabbage, these compounds have been associated with potential anti-cancer effects and cardiovascular benefits. Terpenes (Fig. 5 ): A large and diverse class of compounds found in essential oils and aromatic plants, terpenes were reported to possess anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and neuroprotective properties.

Lysozyme has long been employed as a model protein system in biophysical studies, shedding light on the tricky mechanisms underlying protein aggregation. Researchers have harnessed this system to unravel the impacts of diverse chemical properties, occurrence patterns, and modes of action on lysozyme amyloidogenesis. Polyphenols, encompassing tannins, flavonoids, phenolic acids, coumarins, and stilbenes, have been extensively examined for their potential to mitigate amyloid aggregation of lysozyme. The observed and reported interactions between lysozyme and various phytochemicals are mentioned in the Table 3 . This table gives an insight into the various phytochemicals, their classification, subclass, and the type of interaction, that are being initiated in stabilizing the protein structure. Among these, -epicatechin gallate a commonly found phytochemical in various plant sources, including tea, fruits, and cocoa stands out as an especially potent polyphenol, demonstrating the capability to inhibit fibrillogenesis in hen egg white lysozyme (HEWL). This was achieved under the influence of alkali-salt conditions [110]. The oxidized form of -epicatechin gallate displays approximately 37.4% higher relative potency and ∼ 9.6% enhanced fibril-disruptive potential in vivo compared to its native counterpart [111]. Myricetin is a flavonoid, a type of polyphenolic compound found abundantly in various plants, fruits, and vegetables. It is recognized for its potential health benefits and biological activities. Its molecular structure consists of several hydroxyl groups, enabling it to scavenge free radicals and reduce oxidative stress. Interaction between myricetin and monomeric HEWL significantly suppresses fibril formation, with higher doses of myricetin exhibiting more pronounced inhibitory effects [112]. Extended incubation of 25 μM myricetin with HEWL results in reduced amyloid fibril formation after 12 days, and the addition of 100 μM myricetin leads to the development of amorphous aggregates with minimal HEWL fibrils. Investigations into the conformational changes in HEWL encompassed two distinct molecular dynamic simulation scenarios: one favoring amyloid fibril formation (338K and pH 2.0) and the other simulating normal cellular physiological conditions (310K and pH 7.0). Myricetin's interaction with HEWL was predominantly observed between the α-domain and the β-domain. Notably, residues including Asn44, Asn46, Asp52, Asn59, Glu35, Ala107, Val109, Trp62, and Trp63 were identified as key binding sites for myricetin. This interaction involved a network of hydrogen bonds and Van der Waals interactions. The influence of myricetin on residues Trp62 and Trp63 in the β-domain could elucidate the observed quenching of intrinsic fluorescence [113].

Kaempferol, a natural flavonol, at higher kaempferol/HEWL ratios of 5:1, was found to decrease the aggregation rate by binding at the 10Å active site identified by the co-crystal NAG multimer. The amino acids within the binding groove of residue numbers 60–80 and 90–120 showed the highest atomic variations. Kaempferol established hydrogen bonds with Asp52, Leu56, Asn59, Trp62, and Trp63. It also interacted electrostatically with Asp52 and Asp101 and Van der Waals interaction with Trp62, Trp63, and Asn103 [114]. Curcumin, a naturally occurring phytochemical was reported to reduce almost 80% growth rate of fibril formation. Curcumin interacted with Asp52, Trp62, and Trp63 through hydrogen bonds, Van der Waals interactions with Trp62 and Arg73, and electrostatic interactions with Asp48, Trp63, and Arg70 [114]. Curcumin pre-treatment at 25°C for 7 or 24 days had a stronger inhibitory impact on the development of HEWL fibrils when the curcumin concentration was increased from 10 to 50 M [115]. Both kaempferol and curcumin docked well in the hen egg white lysozyme's hydrophobic groove. Their phenyl rings connected to Trp62's indole ring to form a π–π structure [114].

Rottlerin prevented protein aggregation into highly ordered fibrils of a model protein lysozyme at 10-fold molar excess concentration. Rottlerin attaches close to the amyloidogenic domains of lysozyme proteins, preventing their aggregation. At pH 12.2, rottlerin was discovered to be a HEWL intrinsic fluorescence quencher. The loss of ThT fluorescence on the addition of rottlerin was due to fibril disaggregation into oligomeric forms. Therefore, it changes the fibril's secondary structure to one that is non-β-sheet rich, destabilizing the fibril in the process. This suggests that HEWL and rottlerin have a hydrogen bonding or Van der Waal interaction with -C=O, -C–N, and -N–H groups of HEWL [116–118].

In a documented study, sennoside A (SA) and sennoside C (SC) inhibit the aggregation of Human lysozyme in a concentration-dependent fashion by lowering the β-sheet supplemented fibrillar structure significantly. The inhibitory impact of SA and SC on HL amyloid fibrillation is determined by the effectiveness with which SA and SC bind to the distinctive pocket of HL. These binding forces were identified as hydrogen bonds, Van der Waals interactions, and π-π stacked interactions via the respective associated amino acid residues. There are many interactions between SA and SC and the amino acid residues of HL. In particular, the hydroxy-methyl group in SC builds a direct hydrogen bond with Asn46, whereas the corresponding carboxyl group in SA might generate detrimental negative-negative interactions with Asp49 of HL. HL's Glu35, Asn46, Asp49, Ile59, Tyr63, Trp64, Gln104, Ala108, Trp109, and Arg115 amino acid residues may interact directly with SA [117]. Trp64, Trp109, Asp53, Glu35, and Ala108 of HL's amino acid residues were the key active binding sites for establishing the hydrogen bonds, Van der Waals, and π-π bonds in both SC and SA [55].

Ascorbic acid binds to HEWL at pH 7.4 (50 mM potassium phosphate buffer containing 100 mM NaCl), and at a 1:15 M ratio with HEWL, it virtually entirely prevents the production of amyloid [123]. At a 15-fold molar excess of ascorbic acid, the maximum fluorescence intensity was attenuated by about 97%, increasing the lag time from 20 h to 125 h. Additionally, at a 1:15 HEWL/ascorbic acid ratio, the growth rate dropped from 0.85 fluorescence units per hour to 0.03 fluorescence units per hour [123]. Ascorbic acid prevented the conversion of HEWL to amyloid fibrils but could not stop the partial unfolding of the protein, and may bind strongly to partially unfolded lysozyme, stabilizing and trapping them in that conformation and preventing them from undergoing further unfolding, according to the analysis of ANS fluorescence emission values at 485 nm upon binding to HEWL incubated for 192 h at 55 °C [123]. Ascorbic acid interacts with both the α and β domains at the cleft of HEWL at Trp 108, Ala 95, Leu 56, Gly 104, Met 105, Trp 63, Ala 107, Ile 98, Trp 28, and Gln 57 to bind to the protein, generating an H-bond and a strong Van der Waals contact. At 140M (1:1 M ratio of HEWL: Ascorbic acid), the ThT fluorescence test shows a ∼ 78% suppression of HEWL amyloid fibrillation [123].

Studies on how hesperidin, a flavanone glycoside, interacts with monomers of lysozyme from hen egg whites and the effects this interaction has on the protein's conformation, thermal stability, and aggregation. At the 10Å active site, which is determined by the attached ligand chitopentose, active hydroxyl groups of HESP interact with amino acids Asn46, Asp52, Trp62, Trp63 directly by hydrogen bonds, Asp101, Glu35, Asp48, Gln57 primarily through water bridges, and Arg112, Trp62 hydrophobically [119]. The decrease of α-helical content of HEWL with increasing HESP concentrations implies that HESP binds to the polypeptide chain's amino acid residues and causes conformational changes in the protein molecule which results in total prevention of aggregation reactions at higher concentrations (500 μM) of HESP. The tertiary structure of lysozyme passes through a collaborative thermal unfolding phase with Tm of 74.3°C and 76.4°C, respectively, in both the absence and presence of HESP [119].

Glycyrrhizic acid (GA) is the main bioactive component of the glycyrrhiza rhizome. It is a triterpenoid saponin, a glycoside conjugation of glycyrrhetinic acid and glucuronic acid. GA has been shown to have the ability to prevent HEWL from forming amyloid at 3–4 M GuHCl concentrations at pH 6.3 and 37°C [120]. The signals from the three tinctorial probes decreased by almost 90% above a [GA]/[HEWL] ratio of 2:1, indicating a nearly complete suppression of fibril synthesis. This indicates that in the presence of [GA]/[HEWL] 2:1, a substantial fraction of HEWL was still soluble. Circular dichroism was used to identify the secondary structures of GuHCl-induced HEWL aggregate product in the absence and presence of GA at 2:1 and 4:1 [GA]/[HEWL] ratios. Native HEWL at pH 6.3 exhibits negative peaks at 209 nm and a shoulder at 222 nm in its far UV-CD spectra, which are characteristic of its α+β structure. Between 215 and 220 nm, HEWL fibrils exhibit a single negative band that corresponds to extended β-rich structures [55,120]. Native HEWL has around 30% helix, unfolded HEWL contains approximately 20% helix whereas in final solutions mainly comprised of 2:1 and 4:1 [GA]/[HEWL] ratios, nearly 29% helix content was estimated. The monomeric native was nearly similar in the absence and presence of GA. This implies that the unaggregated soluble protein is primarily monomeric or small oligomeric in nature and further evidence confirms that no oligomers were visible on the TEM grid of a sample with [GA]/[HEWL] = 4 [125]. To assess the GA binding location on HEWL, post-simulation studies and docking were carried out. The binding gap of HEWL was found to include GA partly swinging or hanging from it. Typical hydrogen bonds with residue segments 33–37, 48, and 59, as well as a salt bridge with residue 114, can be found in the HEWL bound to chitopentaose crystal structure with a binding energy of - 32.22 kJM−1. GuHCl-induced hydrogen bonds were found with residues 48, 59, 63, 107 and salt bridges with residues 114 in the HEWL in 2.5 M GuHCl solution crystal structure with binding energy - 34.31 kJM−1. Native HEWL exhibits a red-shift caused by GA at ∼342 nm, with maxima (λmax) at 344 nm and at 346 nm for 2:1 and 4:1 [GA]/[HEWL] samples, respectively. At both ratios, GA exhibits a ∼ 1 nm slight red shift, and the λmax of unfolded HEWL was at ∼355 nm [120].

Moreover, residues like Asp52, Gln57, Asn59, Trp62, Trp63, Ile98, Asn103, Ala107 and Trp108 can inhibit its catalytic activity. Trp62 is a key residue for the fibrillation process and lies in the amyloidogenic aggregation-prone region. Inhibition of amyloid formation as luteolin binds near the Trp62 residue. Interactions between luteolin and amino acids by hydrogen bonding help the luteolin molecule to be packed inside the cleft region of HEWL [126]. Entacapone targets the aggregation-prone region of HL by binding with the residues Glu35, Asp53, Gln58, Trp 64, Ala108, and Trp109 via hydrogen bonding bonds, Van-der-Waals forces, and hydrophobic interactions [127].

The detection and identification of aggregate formation was accomplished by a variety of elements, which aids in the understanding of the structures. When lysozyme is incubated in glycine, it aggregates, and forms amyloid fibrils. Changes in the intensity of Thioflavin-T fluorescence were used to investigate lysozyme fibrillization since the signal obtained in the presence of amyloid fibrils is much higher than the signal received in the absence of amyloid fibrils in native lysozyme samples. As an alternate technique, the fluorescence of 1-anilinonaphthalene-8-sulfonate may also be analyzed analogously. The samples were then visualized for the presence of fibrils using TEM and AFM. The use of a computational approach to explore the link between Hen Egg-White Lysozyme and chosen chemicals via docking studies allows a deeper understanding of these compounds' interaction and binding behaviors. By employing such biophysical tools a recent study indicates that the binding of resveratrol to early-stage Human Lysozyme conformations stabilizes them, preventing fibril formation. This effect is attributed to a reduction in partially folded conformers, recognized as precursors to fibrillogenesis. Resveratrol facilitates the adoption of alternative conformations, preserving α-helical structure and activity, and diverting the pathway to form less-hydrophobic, less-toxic aggregates. In the current scenario to ease the process, (Fig. 6 ) virtual screening can also be used to pool out the phytochemicals that can influence the aggregation properties of lysozyme using advanced high-throughput screening assays [128].

To enhance our understanding of the mechanisms and mode of action of phytochemicals on lysozyme aggregation, a comprehensive investigation was performed. Nine previously unexplored phytochemicals were subjected to virtual screening against both native human lysozyme (PDB ID: 1REX) and hen egg white lysozyme (PDB ID: 193L) using AutoDock Vina, a tool seamlessly integrated within Pyrx. The strategy involved the application of blind docking within substantial search spaces of 52,507.20 Å3 for 1REX and 54,196.99 Å3 for 193L to identify potential binding sites. Binding affinities, quantified in kcal/mol, were carefully assessed to rank the binding poses of these phytochemicals as shown in Fig. 7 and Tables 4 and 5.

Remarkably, the findings unveiled that all the investigated phytochemicals engaged with distinct three-dimensional regions within both 1REX and 193 L, effectively occupying an approximate volume of 3500 Å3. Particularly noteworthy was the exceptional performance of oleuropein, which exhibited the most negative Gibbs free energy during its interactions with both 1REX and 193 L (Fig. 8 a&b) and Table 4 and 5. This superior binding affinity of oleuropein was attributed to its ability to establish a more extensive network of conventional hydrogen bonds with 1REX and 193 L. These outcomes strongly suggest that oleuropein may possess the highest efficacy in inhibiting aggregation among the various phytochemicals under examination. “From this concise study, our observations underscore the necessity for phytochemicals to exhibit robust interactions with specific aggregation-prone regions within lysozyme. Moreover, their capability to effectively engage with the local microenvironment and adapt to diverse structural conformations appears to be critical for maintaining optimal protein functionality under conditions of molecular and cellular stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other natural compounds influence lysozyme aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to phytochemicals, various natural compounds have demonstrated promising interactions with lysozyme, effectively preventing its transition into toxic amyloid structures. Notably, biomolecules such as amino acids, organic acids, neuromodulators, and short peptides, as listed in Table 6 , have exhibited significant potential in this regard. Mainly, these molecules demonstrate the capability to maintain monomeric conformations by interacting with aggregation-prone stretches in both Hen Egg White and Human Lysozyme. Addressing the primary nucleation mechanism at physiological pH, whether by preventing the mechanism or stabilizing its elements, compounds such as succinic acid, citric acid, maleic acid, tartaricacid, and amino acids like phenylalanine, arginine, and lysine have proven effective in inhibiting lysozyme aggregation. [104,129]. Another instance where hydrophobic interactions and hydrogen bonding have been known to interfere with the aggregation process in 3,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid (3,4DHB), cholic acid (CA), taurine-conjugated cholic acid (TCA), lithocholic acid (LCA) and several sugar nanoparticles like ribose, galactose, ficoll, dextran, and polytrehalose. [130–132]. Substances like allantoin, functionalavicilin amyloid, carnosine dipeptide (β-alanine and L-histidine), and neuromodulators like dopamine and hydroxytyrosol have been known to denature the proteins partially, prevent the unfolding process, and induce conformational changes of β-sheets to α helices. [133–136]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations of phytochemicals in preventing lysozyme aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: Phytochemicals, while readily available, often suffer from low bioavailability and limited targeting capabilities, leading to delayed effects. To address these challenges, nanoparticles have been strategically incorporated, offering an enhanced drug delivery system with controlled-release properties that enable specific targeting. They provide excellent versatility, as their structure, and material can be optimized as necessitated by the therapeutic outcomes. This integration of nanoparticles has the potential to overcome the limitations of phytochemicals, improving their efficacy and applicability.

Another family of chemicals used at a 1:37 M ratio were polyols which can hinder the development of aggregates and the unfolding of lysozyme [138]. However, there are disparities in the process by which this occurs as there's no correlation between inhibition of aggregate formation and specific activity loss. Significant structural alterations occurred in lysozyme in the absence of polyol additives. While certain additives, such as lactulose and fructose, improved lysozyme structure, recovery, and specific activity under the circumstances studied, others, such as sucrose and trehalose, caused even greater denaturation and were ineffective in preventing lysozyme aggregation. In an aqueous solution, native lysozyme and unfolded lysozyme remain in equilibrium and because of its amphiphilic nature, lysozyme may adsorb to the interface and was only detected in the presence of this interface. Therefore, it's reasonable to presume that unfolding happens because of lysozyme adsorption at the interface, which is followed by the development of non-covalent aggregates. Alternately, any unfolded species of lysozyme may well be changed by an unknown mechanism, resulting in the formation of unfolded lysozyme [138]. Exhibition of significant inhibitory as well as disaggregation effects on Hen Egg White Lysozyme amyloids and interaction with the phytochemicals were also associated with considerable conformational changes in HEWL. As polyols typically result in significant increases in the Tm values at rather high concentrations of the additive, only minor variations were observed when the additives lactulose (Tm 76 °C), lactose (Tm 75 °C), sorbitol (Tm 76 °C), and sucrose (Tm 75 °C) were used at a 1:37 M ratio of lysozyme to additive [138,139].

Trehalose interacts through hydrophobic interaction with Gln(121), Ile (124), Arg (125), Arg (128) and Leu (129). It interacts through hydrogen bonding with Gly (126) and Cys (127). Compounds may prevent aggregation by blocking the exposed hydrophobic surface in HEWL. Trehalose was found to interact with the amyloidogenic regions in the protein through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions and thereby blocking its aggregation There by converting the β sheets into α -helical conformer [124].

Silybin has been utilized to regulate fibrillogenesis, and the variations between two silybin diastereomers were revealed by their stereoselective metabolisms [140]. The interactions between HEWL and the hydroxyl groups on C20 and C23 were extremely limited, and no hydrogen-bonding interactions with HEWL were observed. Silybin B is metabolized 2–3 times faster than Silybin A, and because both diastereomers influence each other's metabolism, using the two concurrently may reduce their actual effects due to the hindrances that will generate between the diastereomers [141]. The interaction between C20 and the receptor, as well as the groups on the same aromatic ring, may explain the difference in the inhibitory effect of silybin diastereomers on lysozyme fibrillogenesis. Despite having small combination differences between the two diastereomers with HEWL, it was revealed that silybin B has stronger interactions than silybin A [141].

Certain combinations of extensively researched polyphenols can work synergistically to prevent the formation of amyloid aggregates and are also useful for depolymerizing the formations. The binding of every polyphenol toward a distinct amino acid sequence inside the protein may account for variations in these actions. Studies show that the favorable binding of polyphenols to the identified amyloidogenic regions in the lysozyme chain is just what contributes to the synergistic and antagonistic anti-amyloid activity [142]. There is a growing understanding that while natural product combinations from dietary sources entail synergistic, additive, and antagonistic effects, assessments of single components are not necessarily sufficient to accurately identify the health benefits [143].

The most researched plant polyphenols with potential anti-amyloid action [144,145] include tea, curcumin, coumarins, quercetin derivatives, and stilbenes, particularly trans-resveratrol, which is a well-known component of red wine. Resveratrol (R), quercetin (Q), and caffeic acid (C), three structurally distinct red wine phenolics, were discovered to have the potential to depolymerize preformed lysozyme amyloid fibrils (reported as DC50) and inhibit lysozyme amyloid aggregation (represented as IC50) in vitro. The thioflavin-T fluorescence test was used to determine how these polyphenols and their binary or tertiary equimolar combinations affected the in vitro amyloid aggregation of hen egg white lysozyme. The median depolymerization concentrations (DC50) and median inhibition concentrations (IC50) were used to assess the capacity to prevent amyloid from self-assembling. Depending on the method, mixed-sample analyses revealed either synergistic or antagonistic behavior. In the depolymerization of lysozyme amyloid formations, three of the four evaluated equimolar combinations demonstrated a substantial synergic effect, and two of the four also inhibited the development of lysozyme amyloid aggregates [121]. The depolymerizing activity was determined following a 24-h incubation at 37°C of 10 M at pH 2.7 of preformed lysozyme amyloid fibrils with polyphenols or their mixes at concentrations ranging from 10 pM to 1 mM. At the specified excitation wavelength, the polyphenols show no fluorescence. This combination was discovered to degrade lysozyme aggregation in a concentration-dependent way. The inhibitory actions of all equimolar combinations differed significantly from those of depolymerization. At 50% activity, CI values indicate a synergistic impact for Q + R and R + C. Resveratrol was highly effective in both processes. It seemed to be less effective in inhibiting the forming of lysozyme amyloid aggregates than in inhibiting the depolymerization of lysozyme amyloid aggregates, and its meta-dihydroxy substituted A-ring interacts with the hydrophilic region of lysozyme trimer and forms a monohydroxy substituted stilbene B-ring with the hydrophobic region of trimeric lysozyme cavity. On the contrary, mixes Q + C and Q + R + C work antagonistically, including quercetin involvement in lysozyme fibril depolymerization demonstrating about a tenfold reduced effectiveness for aggregation prevention. It favors A-ring association with a hydrophobic area and ortho-dihydroxy substituted flavonoid B-ring interaction with the same hydrophobic region of the trimeric lysozyme cavity [121].

The binding regions for quercetin & and resveratrol were similar when the lysozyme hexamer docked with polyphenols, in contrast to the binding site for caffeic acid. The 1–48/102–129 non-amyloidogenic section of the nicked protein is split from the mature amyloid fibrils, which are mostly made up of 49/53–101 fragments [146,147]. It was discovered that the β-domain and the α-helix, around Gly49-Asp101, are the most amyloidogenic areas inside the hen egg white lysozyme, which may help to explain the antagonistic and synergistic effects. The HEWL monomer binding regions for quercetin are Glu35/ Asn44-Trp62 Ala107-Ala110, whereas for resveratrol and caffeic acid, they share similar active binding sites of Trp62-Leu75 Ile98-Val107 [121]. When it comes to the primary amyloidogenic region, quercetin is surrounded by distinct amino acids, but resveratrol and caffeic acid are associated with the same site. The lysozyme areas that are associated with the production of the amyloid core bind across all three chemicals. The simultaneous creation of a hydrogen bond among Q and C (the hydrogen atom in the caffeic acid's 4-OH group and the oxygen atom in quercetin's 3’-OH group) may be one factor contributing to the antagonistic effects of Q + C and Q + R + C mixes. This interaction could prevent Q and C from interacting with the appropriate amino acid sequences in the most effective manner. On the other hand, the interaction between the Q + R combination and the hydrophobic groups that cause an aggregation is what accounts for the maximum synergy [146]. The synergic effect is also caused by the combined action of R and C in the same lysozyme cavity with a relatively similar hydrophobic group. Interestingly, the combination of these two chemicals with the best IC50 values, R + C, may not be the most successful binary mixture, but rather the combination of Q + R. This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that the Q + R combination restricts a larger lysozyme hydrophobic region than the R + C combination [121].

The effects of Brilliant Blue G (BBG) at lower concentrations show a significantly large decrease in ThT fluorescence intensity detected in a 1-day-aged BBG-containing lysozyme sample [148]. BBG at high concentrations could severely interfere with the binding of ThT to amyloid fibrils, leading to a significant bias in the ThT fluorescence reading of lysozyme samples co-incubated with BBG as BBG occupies more ThT binding sites on amyloid fibrils. TEM findings demonstrate fibrillar species with shorter lengths in the sample solutions upon the addition of BBG under the conditions of pH 2.0 and 55 °C. β-sheet secondary structure in the 1-day aged lysozyme samples indicates no obvious trend regarding the effect of BBG concentration on the percentages of β-sheet secondary structure in lysozyme samples [148]. The addition of BBG caused truncation of the lengths of lysozyme fibrillar species but could not suppress the increase in β-sheet content, and that shorter lysozyme fibrillar species were generated upon the addition of BBG, it was conjectured that the attenuation of both ANS fluorescence intensity and blue shift observed in the BBG-containing samples was likely attributed to the interaction between BBG and hydrophobic regions of lysozyme. Possible interaction modes were reported to be mainly hydrophobic Pi-cation, Pi-alkyl, Pi-Pi stacked interactions, conventional hydrogen bonds, carbon‑hydrogen bonds, electrostatic interactions, and Van Der Waal interactions [148].

The inhibitory effect of Captopril (CAP) on human lysozyme amyloid fibrils and the specific binding was explored, and it was reported that CAP could significantly inhibit HL amyloid fibrils in a dose-dependent manner after incubation at 60 °C for 168 h. This is further supported by the captopril concentration leading to 50% inhibition of amyloid aggregation (IC50) evaluated as 34.75 ± 1.23 μM, which is a measure to quantify the inhibitory activity. Thermodynamic parameters state that the negative value of ΔG° shows the interaction to be a spontaneous process. The negative ΔH° and ΔS° values indicate that the interactions are mainly driven by enthalpy rather than entropy which always exhibits decreased binding constants with the increase of temperature [149]. In addition, the negative enthalpy and entropy changes imply that the binding is mainly driven by hydrogen bond interactions. The stable complex may help maintain the α-helical structure of HL, thereby inhibiting its transition to amyloid fibrils. CAP binds to the active pocket of HL with a free energy of −6.15 kcal/mol, which suggests that CAP could bind to HL spontaneously and form a stable protein−ligand complex. The amino acid residues of Glu 35, Asp 53, Gln 58, Asn 60, Trp 64, Val 99, Ala 108, Trp 109, and Val 110 were involved in the interaction. Glu 35 and Ala 108 form hydrogen bonds and the reduction in the solvent accessibility of Trp 64 and Trp 109 leads to the quenching of the intrinsic fluorescence. The binding of CAP near amyloidogenic regions of HL might be one of the major reasons for its anti-amyloidogenic behavior [47].

Five compounds comprising of di- or tri-substituted benzene derivatives, selected from a library of approximately 50 chemically related organic compounds known for their inhibitory activities against Aβ aggregation [150] and their ability to protect hippocampal neurons in primary culture from Aβ-induced neurotoxicity [150], were investigated for their efficacy in disrupting preformed amyloid fibrils in human and hen lysozyme. The compounds are 3,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid (3,4 DHB), 2-hydroxy-3-ethoxybenzaldehyde (2H3EB), 4-aminophenol (4AP), 4-aminoanisole (4AA), and 2-amino-4-chlorophenol (2A4CP). These compounds were reported to be non-toxic to neurons in culture when used at concentrations up to 100 μM [150]. Out of all the compounds, 2A4CP achieved maximum disaggregation at a 1:1 M ratio to the lysozyme monomers concentration. Further analysis showed that 4AP and 2A4CP disaggregate amyloid fibrils from both hen and human lysozyme at micromolar concentrations. The rationale behind this study was to identify compounds that are small enough to penetrate the fibril structure in order to destabilize both the β-sheet hydrogen bonds of the fibril core and the hydrophobic interactions between amino acid side chains [130].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical inhibitors targeting lysozyme amyloid fibrillation
ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic inhibitors (Table 7 ) have been extensively studied for their protective effect against amyloid fibril formation of both Hen Egg White Lysozyme (HEWL) and Human Lysozyme (HL). The inhibitory mechanisms involve the destabilization of secondary β-sheet structures and disulfide bridges within the proteins. Simultaneously, these compounds demonstrate the capability to diminish the exposure of hydrophobic regions while preserving the overall structural integrity of the lysozyme. This dual mode of action significantly retards the inactivation rate, leading to the formation of a stable complex between the inhibitor and lysozyme. Notably, these inhibitors like moxifloxacin [151] exhibit selective binding to the native conformers of lysozyme, safeguarding its original structure from transitioning into intermediate forms prone to aggregation. Additionally, synthetic inhibitors like 2-hydroxy-3-ethoxybenzaldehyde (2H3EB), 4-aminophenol (4AP), 4-aminoanisole (4AA), 2-amino-4-chlorophenol (2A4CP), toluidine blue O (TBO), methylene blue (MB), poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG), captopril (CAP), rifampicin, p-benzoquinone, tartrazine, amaranth, sunset yellow (SY), ponceau 4R (P4R), thiosemicarbazide, isothiosemicarbazide, cyclic 1,3-thiazolidinone subclasses [47,130,152–157] achieve their inhibitory effects through the destabilization of secondary β-sheet structures and disulfide bridges. Targeting the ability to reduce the exposure of hydrophobic regions while maintaining the integrity of the protein is a key mechanism exhibited in N,N′, N″-triacetylchitotriose (chitotriose), polysulfobetaine, SDS, citraconylated-lysine, (poly(phenylene vinylene)-NMe3+), and clofazimine [158–163]. This dual mechanism contributes to slowing down the rate of inactivation in benzyl alcohol [164]. Furthermore, it was observed that tacrine–coumarin heterodimers and moxifloxacin selectively bind to the native conformers of lysozyme, thereby protecting its native structure from conversion into intermediate structures and preventing self-association [151,165].

Dithiothreitol (DTT) serves as a reducing agent recognized for its capacity to hinder amyloid formation in Hen Egg White Lysozyme (HEWL) at pH 2.4. However, when utilized in conjunction with urea, a denaturant, at pH 7.4, it induces aggregation and the generation of larger aggregates. In native proteins, structural integrity is preserved by native disulfide bonds. During the fibrillation process of HEWL, the aggregation pathway involves the development of non-native intramolecular disulfide bridges [166].
