The introduction of DTT significantly diminishes fibril formation by disrupting these non-native disulfide bonds. Conversely, when DTT is combined with urea at pH 7.4, it collaborates with urea to rapidly unfold HEWL, accelerating the formation of amyloid fibrils.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of acridine derivatives on lysozyme aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: Acridine derivatives are a class of organic compounds that are structurally related to acridine, a three-ring aromatic molecule consisting of two benzene rings fused to a pyridine ring. Acridine derivatives are formed by modifying the acridine structure through various chemical substitutions and functional group additions. These modifications can alter their properties and make them suitable for a wide range of applications in chemistry and biology.

Some common acridine derivatives and their uses include aminoacridines, acriflavine, acridine orange, methylene blue, quinacrine, ethacridine lactate, and proflavine. These compounds are valued for their diverse applications, including their ability to intercalate DNA, which has made them important in molecular biology, genetics, and potential drugs. They are also used in analytical chemistry and various medical and industrial processes.

A screening of nineteen structurally distinct acridine derivatives was conducted to report for inhibition activity of amyloid fibril formation. According to the structural similarity, acridine derivatives can be divided into planar acridines, spiroacridines, and tetrahydroacridines. Most of the planar acridines caused an extensive decline in ThT fluorescence, indicative of their ability to inhibit lysozyme amyloid aggregation. The effect of spiroacridines on the inhibition of lysozyme polymerization was very weak and tetrahydroacridines did not influence the prevention of lysozyme fibrillization [167]. In fact, a few tetrahydroacridine derivatives were shown to promote lysozyme aggregation significantly. The highest inhibition activity was detected for a dimeric planar acridine, characterized by relatively high inhibiting activity at low micromolar concentrations, implying that the potency of acridine compounds can be enhanced by producing covalent acridine dimers. The simplest interpretation of this intensive inhibiting activity is that the duplication of heterocycles in dimeric acridine increases the capacity of the compound to interact with protein leading to more effective blockage of the β-structure formation [167].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of peptide fragments on lysozyme aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: Peptide fragments are short chains of amino acids that are derived from the enzymatic digestion or cleavage of larger proteins or peptides. These fragments typically consist of a sequence of amino acids that is shorter than the parent protein or peptide from which they originated. The potential of three peptide fragments (Lz-peptides) derived from the human lysozyme sequence was explored through the effects of charge and hydrophobicity displaying an anti-amyloid activity, which was mainly due to the lysine residue in Lz-K peptide replacing both Lys and Trp residues in Lz-KW peptide. The largest decrease in fluorescence intensities was noted for the LzK-peptide and the values for Lz-peptide were slightly higher indicating ∼40% of inhibiting activity. On the other hand, the intensities for LzKW-peptide ranging from 90 to 100% indicate minimal to no inhibitory action. In the case of Lz and LzK peptides, the fluorescence signal decreased in plateau phases detecting about 60% and 50% of fluorescence respectively, suggesting that Lz and Lz-K peptides can influence the formation of lysozyme amyloid aggregates due to their ability to inhibit the formation of amyloid fibrils to a certain extent as denoted by the fluorescence signal which was lower than that observed for amyloid aggregates alone. These results further infer that the largest inhibiting activity was observed for the LzK-peptide and the smallest for the LzKW-peptide, which agrees with the data from the ThT assay [168].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Can polysaccharides prevent lysozyme amyloid formation
ce:section.ce:para: Polysaccharides are complex carbohydrates made up of multiple sugar (monosaccharide) units linked together in long chains. They are one of the major types of carbohydrates and serve various essential functions in living organisms. Even polysaccharides were studied for their interaction with lysozyme in stabilizing them. Gum Arabic(GA), arabinogalactan-protein (AGP), poly norbornene glucose ester (PNGE), poly norbornene glucosamine (PNGA), and have the potential to inhibit the amyloid fibrils of Bovine Insulin(BI), Hen Egg White Lysozyme(HEWL) and Aβ in the invitro conditions. They hypothesized that these macromolecules maintain the native folded state or helical intermediates of the proteins BI, HEWL, and Aβ. PNGE and PNGA served as more effective inhibitors than AGP because it has several hydrophobic residues that are useful in the interaction between the proteins. The Aβ fibrils are less affected by the inhibitors because it has mostly random coils structures coil structure as opposed to α-helices [169]. Glycogen structure is modified with the introduction of cinnamoyl, benzoyl, or phenylacetyl groups to increase the π-π interactions, which have a strong influence on the amyloid formation of HEWL. GG-CIN2(1.3 mol% cinnamoyl groups per d-glucose unit) and GG-Ph2(2.4 mol% phenylacetyl groups per d-glucose unit) both showed a substantial acceleration impact on the process of amyloid fibril formation. In comparison to the effects of the other modifications, the GG-Ph1 alteration (1.6 mol% phenyl acetyl groups per d-glucose unit) showed a hindering impact [170]. Sucrose and trehalose induce the formation of α-helices conformations and the tertiary structures of thermally denatured HEWL. Thus the addition of these disaccharides increases the stability of the protein and also reduces the hydrophobic interactions between proteins [171].

Phospholipids are a class of lipids that act as a main component in the cell membrane. In a study, phospholipids with long chain (DMPC) and short chain (DHPC) were found to have suppressive effects on lysozyme aggregation. The hydrophobic tails and hydrophilic heads of phospholipid molecules contact with lysozyme, resulting in a reduction in the concentration of the partly unfolded conformations needed for lysozyme to aggregate. The DHPC acts by engaging the phospholipid with the amyloidogenic area of the lysozyme peptide and preventing this region from intermolecular interactions. DMPC is incapable of eliminating lysozyme amyloid fibrils, they inhibit by adsorption of protein monomers on its surface leading to a reduction in the number of unbound protein molecules [172].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of polymers in preventing lysozyme amyloid fibrillation
ce:section.ce:para: Polyethylene glycols (PEGs) exhibit dual behavior in protein processes—enabling or inhibiting aggregation based on proteins and conditions. Under alkali-salt mediation, both PEG20000 (20PEG) and PEG35000 (35PEG) effectively suppress HEWL fibrillation while concurrently reducing the presence of hydrophobic regions, an effect that becomes more distinct at higher PEG concentrations due to potential viscosity changes. Interestingly, 20PEG surpasses 35PEG in inhibiting fibrillation and crowding effects. Moreover, PEGs prevent the formation of β-sheets, increase helicity, and maintain HEWLs native structure [173]. The synthesis of poly(6-O-MMAGlc), a novel polymer derived from methyl-6-O- methacryloyl-α-D-glucoside (6-O-MMAGlc), effectively inhibits the formation of HEWL amyloid fibrils and shows increased effectiveness with increasing dosages. Additionally, poly(6-O-MMAGlc) counters the transformation of HEWL from α-helix-rich conformation to β-sheet structure, resulting in fewer, thinner, and shorter fibrils [174]. These findings underscore PEGs and poly(6-O-MMAGlc) potential in modulating protein conformation and inhibiting amyloid fibrillation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nanomaterials in preventing lysozyme aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between nanoparticles and proteins represents a multifaceted phenomenon with profound implications. Depending on contextual factors, this interaction has the potential to induce structural and functional modifications in proteins, thereby reshaping their behavior. Remarkably, nanoparticles can either facilitate the formation of amyloid structures or act as deterrents, impeding the growth of fibrillar aggregates of particular interest is the pivotal role played by certain metal nanoparticles, often constructed from silver or gold, in modulating amyloid production. These nanoparticles, when introduced at stable concentrations, exhibit a striking ability to mitigate aggregation tendencies. This effect is linked to the disruptive impact they exert on the stabilizing forces operative at the interfaces between proteins and surface molecules on nanostructured surfaces [175]. The evaluation of amyloid aggregate kinetics necessitates the use of Thioflavin-T fluorescence, offering insights into the process of amyloid formation. Furthermore, the characterization of nanocrystals entails comprehensive analytical techniques such as X-ray diffraction and scanning electron microscopy. Complementing these approaches, light scattering methodologies provide a means to assess turbidity changes, enabling the recognition and identification of amyloid alterations.

To explore deeper into the developed amyloid aggregates, advanced analytical methods, including time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, offer valuable insights. During the interaction between proteins and nanoparticles, a transformative event occurs as proteins adsorb onto the nanoparticle surfaces, forming a discernible ‘protein corona’ [176]. This phenomenon adds complexity to the dynamics of nanoparticle-protein interactions. The quantity of lysozyme amyloid fibrils decreased in a dose-dependent manner as magnetic nanoparticle concentration increased, according to previous reports [177]. The circular dichroism spectra of the native protein has a sizable negative band in the 200–240 nm region. Negative bands between 222 and 208 nm and a negative band at 218 nm are the most prominent spectral fingerprints for the α-helix and β-sheet, respectively [178]. A negative band may be seen in the random coil structure at about 195 nm [179]. Through electrostatic screening, the presence of electrolytes and the high ionic strength of the biological medium during in vitro tests might cause nanoparticle aggregation [180]. The size of the nanoparticles affects the surface curvature that governs protein-nanoparticle interaction. Larger surface area nanoparticles have low surface curvature, whereas smaller particles have greater surface curvature. Over the past decade, several studies have focused on understanding the effect of various nanomaterials as shown in Fig. 9 on preventing the aggregation of lysozyme. These nanomaterials include nanoparticles, microspheres, silver nanoparticles, fluorinated nanoparticles [181], magnetic MnFe2O4 nanoparticles [182], fullerenol [183], Fe3O4-based magnetic nanoparticles [184], CdTe nanoparticles [185] and gold nanoparticles [186]. Results from previous observations testify to the fact the inhibitory effect of these listed nanomaterials had various physiochemical parameters as listed in Table 8 . Data suggest that these nanoparticles interact with the aggregation-prone stretches in the protein thereby presenting the primary and secondary nucleation and oligomerization of lysozyme molecules. It is also observed that the size and surface property play a major role in inducing such an effect. Apart from this, these nanomaterials were able to initiate disassembly of the aggregated proteins. This process can release the monomers which in turn eliminate the toxic nature of aggregated lysozyme molecules. The elaborate information on such effect will be discussed in the following paragraphs. (See Table 9.)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Action of gold and silver nanoparticles on lysozyme aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: Gold and silver nanoparticles have shown promise in targeting lysozyme amyloid formation, which is associated with various diseases, including systemic amyloidosis. These nanoparticles offer unique properties that make them potential candidates for amyloid-targeted therapies involving lysozyme amyloid. Here's how gold and silver nanoparticles can be used to target lysozyme amyloid aggregation of lysozyme. Citrate-coated silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been synthesized to form colloids with equivalent metal core sizes [187]. Citrate helps to attain stability and gain functional hydrophobic groups. Mature amyloid fibrils were produced when lysozyme was incubated under fibrillization-inducing conditions with a pH of 2.7, at 65°C, continual stirring at a rate of 1200 rpm in 70 mM glycine buffer, and 80 mM NaCl [188]. The concentration of nanoparticles ranged from 0.01 nM to 10 nM, but the concentration of lysozyme amyloid fibrils was kept constant. Because there are more scattering species present in the solvent and the sedimentation process is slower, incubating mature amyloid lysozyme fibrilization in the presence of 5 nM AuNPs increases the percentage of scattered light. The assembly of the fibrils also enhanced with the increasng concenteration of nanoparticle in a dose dependent manner. At the maximum nanoparticle concentration of 25 nM, it was clear that both components had aggregated to form a complex. Increased nanoparticle concentration enhances the roughness of the surface, which may cause partial loss of fibril structure or depolymerization [178]. The circular dichroism spectra showed the gradual loss of β-sheet contents with the increasing concentration of AuNPs. The secondary structure of the fibrils is lost because of the electrostatic interactions between mature amyloid lysozyme fibrils and AuNPs. This effect can be rationally attributed to the fact that NPs with negative surface charges interact with the fibrilar structure in an efficient manner up to 1 nM, and it is established that at 11 nM, at least 50% of the amyloid structures are degraded. At a concenteration of 100 nM, the about 85% of fibril degradation was observed. Although the AgNPs' lethal dosage was determined to be at 2.5 nM there is no workable concentration range where silver NPs would have an anti-amyloid impact without having a toxic effect since AgNPs are toxic above 1.2 nM but do not have any adequate anti-amyloid action below 11 nM [178].

Proline functionalized gold nanoparticles (Pro-AuNPS) were synthesized by the addition of tri-sodium citrate, which were then aged with AuNPs for many hours. The flat ThT profile of the HEWL-Pro-AuNPs system, which lacked either elongation or saturation phases, supported the inhibitory function of HEWL fibrillation. The decrease in ANS fluorescence intensity suggests that proline and pro-AuNPs were responsible for limiting the hydrophobic residues' exposure. Followed by a 72-h incubation under amyloidogenic circumstances, there was a decrease in α-helix content and an increase in β-sheet content, which suggests the formation of amyloid fibrilar [189,190]. Analysis of the secondary structure of the HEWL-proline system found that proline was not successful in retaining the helical shape of the protein, but greatly prevented the formation of β-sheets. The changes in nature of interaction between proline and HEWL molecules are thought to be the cause of the morphological variations. Proline was observed to bind the residues Ala107, Trp108, Asn52, Gln57, and Asn59 based on the strongest HEWL-proline interaction, which had a binding affinity of −15.19 kcal/mol. It was discovered that proline inhibits HEWL aggregation through hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic contact between the protein and ligand. Additionally, Pro-AuNPs- HEWL interaction was favoured via hydrophobic moieties, leading to conformational alterations and likely restricting oligomer formation [190,191].

Polyvinylpyrrolidone-capped gold nanoparticles were used to study the effects it had on lysozyme amyloid formation. Because PVP binds to the molten globule-like refolding intermediate, shielding the exposed hydrophobic groups seems to be crucial [192]. The PVP-AuNPs induce change in the conformations of HEWL by reduction of β-sheet content and an enhancement of α-helix content. The inclusion of PVP and PVP-AuNPs causes HEWL to adopt a less hydrophobic surface-exposed conformation, as seen by the ANS binding experiment results. Different conformational changes were observed in lysozyme in the presence of PVP. However, when PVP molecules are coupled to gold nanoparticles, the they become more susceptible to aggregation-prone intermediate [193]. It's worth noting that while gold and silver nanoparticles show their potential in targeting lysozyme amyloidosis, more research is needed to ascertain their safety, effectiveness, and clinical viability. The appropriate functionalization and size of nanoparticles play a significant role in their interactions with lysozyme amyloid structures, and their potential as therapeutic tools requires careful evaluation through further studies and clinical trials. Gold and silver nanoparticles, despite their versatility, encounter several limitations. These include high production costs, potential environmental toxicity, stability concerns, challenges in achieving precise size control, and limitations in surface functionalization. These nanoparticles are generally less reactive and can raise environmental and biocompatibility concerns. Ongoing challenges in their application include achieving precise size control and preventing aggregation. It is essential to carefully consider these limitations when exploring the use of gold and silver nanoparticles in various applications.

Iron oxide nanoparticles come in several forms, including magnetite (Fe3O4) and hematite (Fe2O3), each with its distinct characteristics and applications. One of the most prominent features of these nanoparticles is their magnetic properties, which make them valuable in fields like medicine for targeted drug delivery and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). They can be easily manipulated and guided by external magnetic fields, allowing for precise targeting in biological systems.

Surface-coated iron oxide magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) functionalized with various amino acids (AA), namely with lysine (Lys), glycine (Gly), and tryptophan (Trp), have been extensively studied due to their improved biocompatibility [194,195]. The functionalized AA-MNPs were created by combining MNPs with a solution of studied amino acid (Gly, Lys, or Trp) at room temperature to create the perchloric stabilized iron oxide MNPs, which were produced using the co-precipitation standard protocol [177]. The the IC50 values obtained from the dosedependent inhibition reactions of lysozyme aggregation in the presence of nanoparticles were used to score the antiamyloid efficacy of nanoparticles. The anti-amyloid effect was examined over a wide range of AA-MNP concentrations, and it was revealed that these nanoparticles inhibit lysozyme amyloid aggregation in a dose-dependent manner as evident from the ThT assays. IC50 values of both Gly-MNPs and Lys-MNPs against lysozyme aggregation were observed to be 242.4 g.mL-1 and 283.3 g.mL-1, respectively. Trp-MNPs showed the strongest inhibitory action, and its IC50 value was observed to be the lowest at 125.9 g.mL-1 [177]. These values are less than the IC50 value previously reported for uncoated MNPs, which was 654.0 g.mL-1 [184]. Using the DC50 to measure the effect on lysozyme amyloid fibrils, the values for Gly-MNPs and Lys-MNPs were found to be 95.3 g.mL-1 and 111.0 g.mL-1, respectively. Trp-MNPs were once more shown to have the lowest DC50 value at 83.9 g.mL-1, which corresponds to the strongest fibril disintegration potential [177]. The DC 50 Value of Trp-MNP was observed to be half of the DC 50 value observed for uncoated MNPS [184]. The substantial reductiion in the the fibrilar strucures was observed in the presenc of AA-MNPs and the extent of fibril disintegration was dependent onn the type of aminoacids coated on the MNPs. The presence of Gly-MNPs and Lys-MNPs resulted in the fomration of extremely short and thin fibrils. Trp-MNPs considerably decreased the number of aggregates and only tiny globular aggregates were seen, which suggests substantial inhibition of lysozyme fibrillation [177]. These findings suggest that the amyloid fibrillization of lysozyme can be greatly influenced by superparamagnetic nanoparticles coated with amino acids. Iron oxide nanoparticles come with various limitations, including potential biodegradability concerns, susceptibility to oxidation, challenges in achieving precise size control, variability in biocompatibility, and environmental impact considerations. Their magnetic properties may result in aggregation, posing limitations in non-magnetic applications. Achieving accurate size control and addressing potential toxicity and regulatory considerations are crucial factors that need careful attention when utilizing iron oxide nanoparticles.

Zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles are extremely small, typically ranging in size from 1 to 100 nm (nm). Due to their small size and unique properties, zinc oxide nanoparticles have been used in various fields, including electronics, optics, cosmetics, and medicine. Zinc oxide nanoparticles were synthesized using the chemical precipitation technique [196]. These nanoparticles exhibit a particle size distribution ranging from 10 nm to 100 nm. In colloidal solutions, the ZnO nanoparticles manifest a characteristic color due to their small size, and their absorption spectra display peaks at 375 nm. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) analysis revealed the inhibition effect of ZnO nnoparticles against lysozyme aggregation. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) analysis indicated that lysozyme in the absence of ZnO nanoparticles undergoes and amyloid aggregation process leading to the formation of amyloid fibrils. When lysozyme-to-nanoparticle ratios reached 1:5, the nanoparticles adhered to the aggregated lysozyme, effectively decelerating the aggregation process. Fluorescence spectroscopy analysis revealed that in its native conformation of lysozyme exhibited an emmision maximum of approximately 340 nm. However, in the presence of ZnO nanoparticles, this maxima shifted to around 350 nm, indicating structural alterations in lysozyme. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of tryptophan, a key molecule in lysozyme, decreased significantly in the presence of nanoparticles. This decrease suggests that tryptophan molecules experience a change to a more polar environment upon binding with nanoparticles, signifying substantial conformational shifts in the protein molecules [197]. Also, it is observed that the zinc ions can influence the chemical properties of epigallocatechin-gallate (ECGC) which in turn suppresses the aggregation of human islet amyloid polypeptides (hIAPP) [198]. Zinc oxide nanoparticles exhibit limitations, including potential toxicity concerns, especially at high concentrations, requiring careful consideration of their biocompatibility in specific applications. These nanoparticles may also display limited stability and a tendency to agglomerate, potentially impacting their overall performance. Achieving precise size control and addressing potential environmental impact concerns, particularly in aquatic ecosystems, are crucial considerations when utilizing zinc oxide nanoparticles.

Gold nanorods are elongated nanoparticles made of gold. They have a unique rod-like or cylindrical shape, which distinguishes them from spherical nanoparticles. These nanorods have gained significant attention in the field of nanotechnology and materials science due to their distinct properties, particularly in the realm of optical and biomedical applications. Recent investigations introduced quercetin polymeric nanorods and quercetin-functionalized polymeric gold nanoparticles. These resulting conjugates, termed quercetin-polyvinylpyrrolidone (Q-PVP) and quercetin-polyvinylpyrrolidone‑gold nanoparticles (Q-PVP-Au), exhibited unique rod-like morphologies with narrow dimensions. Their influence on the aggregation of HEWL protein was meticulously examined, revealing dose-dependent inhibitory patterns. Both Q-PVP and Q-PVP-Au demonstrated notable aggregation suppression, with Q-PVP-Au displaying heightened inhibitory potential as evidenced by lower IC50 and DC50 values. Furthermore, Q-PVP-Au induced marked alterations in the morphology of HEWL aggregates, favoring the formation of smaller globular structures [122]. Both gold nanorods (AuNRs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) exhibit inhibitory effects on HEWL protein aggregation. However, AuNRs exert a more pronounced impact owing to their larger surface area, facilitating enhanced protein adsorption. This adsorption phenomenon onto both AuNRs and AuNPs represents the primary mechanism underpinning HEWL fibrillation inhibition. It effectively reduces the protein concentration in the solution, thereby retarding nucleation formation and fibril growth. Additionally, AuNRs promote a more stable α-helix conformation within HEWL molecules and create a hydrophobic microenvironment for the aromatic side groups. Collectively, these factors contribute to the formation of smaller amorphous aggregates, elucidating the dynamics of fibrillation inhibition [199]. Nanorods come with limitations, facing challenges in achieving uniform size and shape that can impact their performance across various applications. The synthesis of nanorods tends to be more complex and costly compared to other nanoparticle shapes. Additionally, owing to their elongated structure, nanorods may exhibit higher anisotropy and encounter alignment issues in certain applications, necessitating careful design and consideration of their orientation.

In a fascinating study, researchers have revealed the impact of Molybdenum Disulphide (MoS2) can be used to make nanosheets and these nanosheets were observed to prevent amyloid fibril formation of hen egg white lysozyme. As the concentration of MoS2 nanosheets increases, there is a notable reduction in lag phases and a significant increase in fibril formations [200]. Interestingly, single-layered MoS2 nanosheets exhibited a higher nucleation rate for protein fibrillogenesis compared to few-layered ones. The interactions between MoS2 nanosheets and lysozyme involve hydrophobic interactions, electrostatic interactions, and Van der Waals forces [200]. The benzene-rings and di-sulfides in lysozyme are believed to form strong hydrophobic interactions with MoS2 nanosheets, while the zeta potential of the nanosheets indicates their propensity to combine with the positively charged lysozyme through electrostatic interactions [200]. At hydrophobic interfaces of the MoS2 nanosheets, the adsorbed lysozyme undergoes structural changes, losing α-helical structure and gaining random-coil and β-sheet structures. These conformational alterations facilitate further binding of proteins from the solution, ultimately accelerating surface-induced protein fibrillation [201]. Another intresting nanosheet material is Tungsten Disulphide (WS2), which is known to exhibits a dual effect on amyloid fibril formation. At pH 2 and when lysozyme is incubated at 20 mg/ml with WS2, there is an increase in the antiparallel β-sheet content. However, at pH 11.5, the antiparallel β-sheet content is reduced, both at high and low WS2 concentrations. These findings suggest that the effect of WS2 on amyloid fibril formation is depedent on temperature and pH [202]. These studies revealed the interplay between nanosheets and lysozyme in the context of amyloid fibril formation, offering valuable insights into the underlying mechanisms and their potential future applications. Nanosheets encounter limitations, facing challenges in large-scale production with consistent thickness and structural integrity. The two-dimensional nature of nanosheets poses difficulties in controlling their orientation and alignment, potentially impacting their performance across various applications. Furthermore, the synthesis methods for nanosheets can be very complex, demanding precise control overgrowth conditions.

It has been reported in research [212], that polymer hydrogel nanoparticles (NPs) are capable of refolding the lysozyme. Denatured lysozyme has a stronger affinity towards negatively charged and hydrophobic NPs. It is anticipated that tiny denatured-lysozyme particles may be enclosed inside hydrogel particles and separated from the external environment. Through intramolecular contact, encapsulated lysozymes can achieve their most stable native conformation without re-aggregation with other denatured proteins. Due to the poor affinity between native lysozyme and NPs, the refolded lysozyme is immediately released from the NPs or replaced by other denatured lysozymes, resulting in the effective refolding of aggregated protein. The findings of Bednarikova and colleagues [205] suggest that smaller nanoparticles of carboxymethyldextran (CMdex NPs) and plain dextran (Dex NPs) slow down the formation of amyloids most likely by modifying the concentration of free monomeric peptides present in a solution after complexation of these monomeric units on the surface diethylaminoethyldextran dextran nanoparticle (DEAE NPs).

Nano-polyphenol epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG) can suppress amyloid aggregation in extracellular space or inside the cell, and dissintegrate mature fibrils, and protect cells from the harmful impact of amyloid aggregates. This nano-EGCG was shown to be 10 times more effective than molecular EGCG [213]. A previous study on gold nanoparticle-decorated quercetin polymer nanorods. [122] demonstrates that Q-PVP and Q-PVP-Au prevent the development of fibrrilar aggregates compared to Q, PVP, and AuNP, which may be related to the distinctive nanorod shape. Polymers employed as nanoparticles face limitations, with potential biocompatibility concerns contingent on the specific polymer type, surface modification and its size. Attaining precise control over their size and uniformity can pose challenges, thereby influencing their overall performance. Certain polymers may experience degradation or loss of stability over time, potentially affecting their long-term effectiveness. Moreover, the release of polymer nanoparticles into the system can give rise to the formation of protein complexes over its surface due to its inherent hydrophobicity which may even trigger a dominos effect.

Hydrogels that are nanoscaled and made of hydrophobic, electrostatic, covalent, and coordinate linkages are known as nanogels. The enormous and tuneable surface areas that emerge from their modest size make it simple to encapsulate various molecules in it. These gels provide a variety of benefits, including excellent biocompatibility and effective penetration [214]. In the first study by Rajan & and Matsumura, the design and synthesis of zwitterionic nanogels using a two-step reaction that involves radical polymerization techniques. It has been observed that nanogels demonstrate significant inhibition activity against lysozyme aggregation, this is due to the retention of higher-order structures which aids in additional stability by acting as molecular chaperones [163]. The main application of nanogels has been in the diagnostics, sensing, and protein delivery fields. With increasing hydrophobicity and molecular weight, the preservation of secondary structures of proteins was more prominently evident in the presence of nanogels [163]. Nanogels present certain limitations, notably encountering challenges in achieving uniform size and structure, potentially affecting their drug delivery and cargo-release capabilities. The synthesis of nanogels may involve complex and specialized procedures. Additionally, concerns regarding the biocompatibility and potential toxicity of nanogels, particularly when certain crosslinking agents are used, require careful consideration in the context of medical and pharmaceutical applications.

They are defined as Nanoparticles (NPs) consisting of spherical matrices, having a diameter of only a few nanometres [215]. These NPs are either crystalline or amorphous in nature having the ability to protect the drug of interest from chemical and enzymatic degradation. Nanospheres have mainly been used in drug delivery thanks to their small size, site-specific targeting, controlled administration, reduced toxicity, and the ability to penetrate the target cells or organs with ease [216]. Therefore, these nanospheres can be potentially used for targeting protein aggregates by delivering aggregation inhibitors such as phytochemicals into the human body, thereby protecting them from getting degraded and also effectively inhibiting the formation of aggregates. For example, a research study elaborates on the potential use of curcumin lysozyme-imprinted nanosphere (CUR-MIMs) to inhibit the amyloid formation of HEWL. The CUR-MIMs can be used for treating neurodegenerative diseases and systemic amyloidosis. These nanospheres were found to have excellent encapsulation efficiency (33.7%) and drug loading capacity for curcumins, theorizing a potentially new strategy for designing drug delivery systems for highly hydrophobic drugs. The CUR-MIMs were found to be highly effective in inhibiting lysozyme misfolding and aggregate formation, with an efficiency of 56.7%. It was hypothesized that CUR-MIMs can bind to lysozyme via hydrophobic interactions and therefore reduce the overall hydrophobicity of the solvent-exposed regions on the lysozyme fibrils. This in turn reduces the overall self-assembly cycle triggered by the hydrophobic interaction of lysozyme [217]. A similar study elaborates on using polymeric conjugates of curcumin such as Curcumin- Polyvinylpyrrolidone Polymer (C-PVPs) and C-PVPs coupled with gold nanoparticles (C-PVP-Au) to prevent HEWL aggregation. Aggregation kinetic studies showed inhibition of protein aggregation, due to the presence of hydroxyl groups in curcumin, which forms H-bonds with the β-sheets present on the lysozyme surface. Notably, C-PVP-Au displayed the best inhibitory potential with improved water solubility provided by the gold nanoparticles [204]. Nanospheres come with limitations, encountering challenges in achieving precise size control and maintaining consistent particle size, potentially impacting their performance in drug delivery and other applications. The synthesis methods for nanospheres can involve highly sensitive procedures, and the choice of materials may influence their biocompatibility and potential toxicity. Furthermore, nanospheres may exhibit limitations in drug-loading efficiency and controlled release, necessitating improvised design tailored to specific applications.

Quantum dots (QDs) are defined as semiconductor particles that are a few nanometres in size and possess optical and electronic properties. QDs have been used in several applications such as nanoshells, nano barcodes, dendrimers, gold colloids, and magnetic tags, due to their high photostability and low biotoxicity [218]. Many case studies have shown increasing potential for utilizing QDs as effective probes and potent inhibitors of protein aggregation and amyloid linked diseases. However, graphene and carbon QDs were studied most extensively due to their advantageous characteristics such as high specific surface area, strong chemical inertness, low toxicity and increased accessibility to incorporate modifications by other adsorbent surface chemicals and nanomaterial, versatility, high quantity of edge sites for functionalization and higher solubility in various solvents. On the other hand, Cadmium QDs were also found to display favorable characteristics such as broad UV excitation, high photostability, and brighter luminescence along with a narrow emission spectrum [219,220]. Additionally, they have been utilized in several other applications such as electrochemical sensors, antibacterial and antioxidant activity, biological imaging, photoluminescence, electro-chemiluminescence, and electrochemical sensors [219]. Quantum dots exhibit limitations, with notable concerns regarding potential toxicity attributed to their heavy metal content, for example cadmium. Challenges in achieving optimal size and uniformity can impact their properties and application effectiveness. The relatively high production costs of quantum dots constrain their widespread utilization remains challenging.

Cadmium Telluride Quantum Dots (CdTe-QDs) are nanoscale semiconductor particles made of cadmium (Cd) and tellurium (Te) atoms, arranged in a crystal lattice structure. The possibility of employing a label-free aptasensor for the fluorescent and visual detection of lysozyme has been explored, utilizing AuNPs, CdTe and QDs as integral components. This innovative approach has shown numerous benefits associated with the incorporation of CdTe-QDs, including enhanced sensitivity, direct visual observation, independence from specialized equipment, specificity toward lysozyme, and the avoidance of modifying AuNPs and using labeled aptamers. [206]. In a separate investigation, N-acetyl-L-cysteine-capped CdTe QDs of varying sizes were utilized to assess their impact on the conformation of HEWL. Through spectroscopic analysis, researchers observed the spontaneous formation of QD-lysozyme complexes, where the size of the QDs played a significant role in inducing pronounced conformational changes to the HEWL structure. These changes led to the unfolding of lysozyme structure, as hydrophobic Trp residues were exposed to the hydrophilic environment. Furthermore, it was revealed that these complexes exhibited inhibitory effects on lysozyme activity, by binding to the enzyme substrate's active site. [221]. This study sheds light on the molecular interactions between CdTe QDs and lysozyme, providing valuable insights into potential applications in biomedical research and beyond.

Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are nano-sized carbon-based materials typically derived from graphene, a two-dimensional sheet of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice. GQDs possess a quantum confinement effect due to their small size, resulting in discrete energy levels and distinct electronic behaviors. These nanomaterials have garnered significant interest in various fields, including biomedicine, electronics, and environmental applications, due to their exceptional properties. GQDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and good water solubility, making them promising candidates for biomedical imaging, drug delivery, and theranostic applications.

Recent study emphasized the impact of Nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (QDs) on the fibrillation of Hen Egg White Lysozyme (HEWL) [207]. These findings revealed that these QDs exhibited inhibitory effects on HEWL fibrillation, even under extreme conditions of elevated temperature (65°C) and acidic pH (pH = 2). Moreover, the study demonstrated a dose-dependent inhibition of HEWL in the presence of Nitrogen-doped Graphene QDs. Similarly, [208] explored the interaction between Graphene Oxide Quantum Dots (GOQDs) and HEWL, focusing on the hydrophobic regions of the protein. The presence of GOQDs was found to considerably delay fibril formation, owing to significant conformational changes at the protein sites, leading to reduced hydrophobicity and decreased β-sheet formation. Interestingly, the inhibitory effect was augmented when GOQDs were functionalized with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, enhancing their electrostatic interaction with lysozyme [211]. Beyond their inhibitory effects on fibrillation, Graphene Quantum Dots have shown potential in facilitating the nucleation of lysozyme monomeric units which further [222] demonstrated that the presence of GQDs reduced the nucleation time significantly, as confirmed by Raman spectroscopy and bright field microscopy analysis. Rigrious examination through confocal fluorescence microscopy revealed the incorporation of GQDs within the lysozyme crystals during their growth in the presence of GQDs. This was then validated by X-ray crystallographic studies which indicated a minimal deviations in the structural parameters of lysozyme crystals grown with or without GQDs. These compelling studies shed light on the diverse applications of Graphene Quantum Dots, ranging from their inhibitory effects on protein fibrillation to their facilitation of crystal nucleation.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered significant attention in the field of biology due to their unique properties and diverse applications. CQDs are nanoscale carbon-based materials, typically less than 10 nm in size, and they possess intriguing optical, electronic, and surface properties. Their biocompatibility, low toxicity, and tunable surface functionalities make them attractive candidates for various biological applications. Due to its versatility, they are also being used to target protein aggregation-based complications. A study by Guerrero and colleagues in 2021, portrayed Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) as remarkable disruptors of lysozyme amyloid aggregates. Through their investigation of the interaction between Na-acetate CQDs and a HEWL fibril model, they unveiled the potent role of this nanoscaled material in combating amyloid formation. Employing cutting-edge techniques like the Thioflavin T (ThT) assay and Atomic Force Microscopy/Dynamic Light Scattering (AFM/DLS) analysis, the researchers witnessed the impressive prowess of Na-acetate CQDs. These CQDs not only prevented amyloid fibril formation by intervening in the structures of monomeric and oligomeric intermediates, but they also orchestrated the dismantling of preformed HEWL fibrils. Such disassembly was witnessed for both monomeric and large fibrillar species, and it was found to be contingent on the increase in Na-acetate CQD concentrations [209]. Recent studies on carbon quantum dots revealed that when Carbon Quantum Nanodots (CQD) are added to HEWL at pH 2.7, they significantly diminish the majority of the fibril population within the pre-fibrillar and fully developed fibrillar species. When combined with HEWL, CQD effectively stabilizes HEWL by elevating the activation energy required for amyloidogenesis, ultimately regulating the course of the aggregation pathway. Furthermore, the efficiency of CQDs decreases as the aggregation pathway advances. [223] The exciting proposition arose that CQDs are deftly bound to the C-terminal region of the fibrils, defying amyloid aggregates. This binding process ingeniously sequestered monomers, oligomers, and protofibrils, rendering them powerless to initiate new fibril seeds, obstructing the treacherous path of oligomerization, and halting the eventual growth of fibrils.

Radiating with their unique optical properties and presenting a beacon of biocompatibility, CQDs shine as promising agents in the realm of biomedical research. The work by Guerrero et al. (2021) illuminates the bright potential of CQDs as safe and novel warriors against amyloid-related diseases. Their non-toxic nature, as also highlighted by Prabhu and Sarkar (2022), adds to the allure of these nanoscale champions in the vibrant and ever-evolving world of science [223].

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are defined as cylindrical nanostructures which are made up of carbon as allotropes. They are made up of carbon atoms in the form of sheets that are 1 atom thick, which are then rolled to become nanotubes. Based on the thickness of the sheets, these carbon nanotubes can further be classified into Single-Walled Nano Tubes (SWNTs) and Multi-Walled Nano Tubes (MWNTs). These nanotubes have shown significant potential in preventing protein aggregation, thanks to their inherent physical and electrical properties such as Rigid structure for mechanical support along with improved nano-scaffolds (especially in neuronal tissues), improved solubility in physiological solvents and effective biocompatibility, therefore expanding their application in neuro-regeneration and drug delivery [210,224]. A study displayed the enhanced antibacterial activity of CNTs when combined with lysozyme (LSZ) and cationic surfactants such as tetradecyl trimethylammonium bromide (TTAB) to form an LSZ–TTAB–SWNT complex, which not only increases the toughness of the fiber but also retains the lysozyme activity by 70% [225,226] reported on the potential application of lysozyme in the formation of SWCNs (Single-Walled Carbon Nanotubes). This study covered the potential applications of SCWN – Lysozyme interactions such as stability of the protein structure, switching between aggregated and dispersed states using pH as a factor, and the design of solution-based optical sensors. [227] studied the adsorption properties of BSA (Bovine Serum Albumin) and Lysozyme using Hydroxylated (HM), Graphitized (GM), and Carboxylated (CM) Multi-Cell Walled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNs). Using the Langmuir model, it was observed that Lysozyme Hydroxylated (LYZ-HM) had the maximum adsorption capacity with BSA-GM having the least, whereas LYZ-GM displayed the highest surface area normalized adsorption capacity, with BSA-CM being the least. [228] elaborated on how AP-LYS (Aminopropyl-Lysozyme), derived from the denatured HEWL, when coated on CNTs will increase the unbundling and solubilization of CNTs, using studies such as zeta-potential electrophoretic mobility and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). This versatile biohybrid material can be utilized as an effective bio-flocculating agent used for treating wastewater, industrial fermentation, and scaffold preparation for tissue engineering (due to their low stability). The study also highlighted the reversible binding between LYS and CNTs can potentially cause no significant alterations to the enzyme's secondary and tertiary structures. There were even studies on lysozyme-CNT hybrid formation and their non-covalent interactions at the nano-bio interface. It was observed that the secondary structure of lysozyme was only slightly affected and that the hybrid interaction between MWCNT and lysozyme increased the rigidity of the spatial structure of lysozyme, thereby improving the overall stability of the protein. Additionally, binding between MWCNTs and the lysozyme was found to be bound near tryptophan residues (Trp 52 and Trp 108), by analyzing the fluorescence emission of lysozyme [224]. However, a study performed by Olenchuk et al. [229], evaluated the role of SWCNTs and MWCNTs in promoting amyloid fibril formation in HEWL under varying pH conditions (2.5, 5.3, 5.5, 6.0, and 11.5), using FTIR spectroscopy and confocal microscopy. These CNTs were later found to induce fibril formation under ideal conditions such as normal room temperature and pH ranging from 5.5 to 6, thus proving to be potentially good promoters of fibril formation in HEWL. These groundbreaking studies underscore the multifaceted interactions between lysozyme and carbon nanotubes, shedding light on their potential implications for enzyme stability and amyloid fibril formation. Such findings are valuable for advancing our understanding of the interplay between proteins and nanomaterials, opening new avenues for nanobiotechnology and biomedical applications. Nanotubes face limitations, encountering difficulties in achieving uniform size, length, and chirality, thereby influencing their properties and potential applications. Some nanotubes may raise health concerns due to their aspect ratio and durability, particularly in scenarios involving inhalation exposure. The synthesis methods for nanotubes can be more complex, often involving catalysts that must be subsequently removed, impacting the purity of the end product. Furthermore, the functionalization and dispersion of nanotubes present challenges, influencing their compatibility within various systems.

Micelles, versatile chemical structures, exhibit an intriguing amphipathic nature, comprising a hydrophobic tail and a hydrophilic head. A significant characteristic of micelles is the Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC), a pivotal parameter that governs their behavior. CMC represents the minimum quantity of surfactants needed to achieve the maximum reduction in surface tension of water. In simpler terms, it signifies the minimum concentration of surfactants required for the spontaneous formation of micelles [230]. The interplay between the hydrophobic and hydrophilic segments within micelles creates a dynamic equilibrium, enabling these structures to efficiently solubilize hydrophobic substances in aqueous environments. This quality makes micelles indispensable in various applications, from enhancing drug delivery in pharmaceuticals to facilitating the homogenization of fats in dairy products. Furthermore, micelles play a fundamental role in the soap industry, aiding in the removal of oil and dirt by forming micelle clusters around the unwanted particles, thus enabling their removal from surfaces. Also, micelles are known to be a well-understood and versatile model system that can mimic the phospholipid membrane. This model system was also used to study the significance of amyloid peptides. Data suggest that the micelle was known to influence and trap the α-helical structures of Islets Amyloid Polypeptide (IAPP) [231].

Case studies have revealed that micelles possess dual roles as potent inhibitors and potential effectors against protein aggregation. In a study by Lindhoud et al. (2007), the possibility of encapsulating enzymes using polymers for stability and activity analysis was explored. Thus Coacervate core micelles were used for encapsulating lysozyme under different mixing ratios and it was observed that not only encapsulation of proteins using enzymes were feasible, but also this makes them suitable for enzymatic applications facilitating the accessability of enzymes in the core of the homopolymer molecules. [232]. The access to enzymes within the core of homopolymer molecules proved beneficial in this context. Similarly, a study [96] has investigated the impact of protonation on lysozyme aggregation using micellar concentrations of Sodium Dodecyl Sulfate (SDS), highlighting the crucial role of electrostatic interactions in lysozyme aggregation. Another study examined SDS's behavior on fibrillation of Hen Egg White Lysozyme (HEWL) at pH 2.0 and various concentrations. At higher concentrations (0.25 mM, 0.50 mM, and 20 mM), SDS was found to suppress fibril formation in HEWL and enhance its thermodynamic stability. In contrast, at lower concentrations (0.00 mM, 0.06 mM, and 0.01 mM), SDS led to the formation of β-sheet-rich conformers in HEWL with decreased thermodynamic stability. These findings highlight the crucial role played by SDS micelles in not only preventing HEWL aggregation at higher concentrations but also preserving the stability of the lysozyme structure even under unfavorable pH conditions [233]. These case studies underscore the multifaceted nature of micelles in protein aggregation processes. Their ability to both inhibit and modulate aggregation behavior adds to their significance in various scientific investigations and potential applications in diverse fields. Micelles' intriguing properties continue to unlock new possibilities in the realm of protein research and beyond. Micelles exhibit limitations, notably their sensitivity to environmental conditions and potential instability over time, which may impact their efficacy as drug delivery vehicles. Attaining precise control over the size and uniformity of micelles poses a challenge. Additionally, the cargo-loading capacity of micelles may be restricted for certain hydrophobic compounds, and controlling cargo release, especially in complex biological environments, can be challenging. Factors like pH and temperature can influence the stability of micelles, emphasizing the need for a precise design tailored to be used for specific applications.

Dendrimers are meticulously structured branched polymeric macromolecules at the nanoscale, featuring well-defined radial symmetry and homogeneity. The classification of dendrimers is based on the process of generating branched chains during their synthesis [234]. These nanoassemblies possess distinctive properties that significantly impact protein aggregation, especially in preventing lysozyme aggregation. Notable features include a substantial number of terminal functional groups, monodispersity, high charge density, and compact nanometer-size dimensions. As promising candidates, dendrimers demonstrate not only anti-amyloid properties but also serve as exceptional models for exploring the structure-activity relationships of protein aggregates [235]. One notable use of dendrimers, such as Poly(aminoamide) (PAMAM) and poly(propyleneimine) (PPI), have played a pivotal role in the effective removal of prion proteins from brains infected with scrapie [236].

Additionally, a study by Tomašovi cová et al. (2018) highlighted the interplay between magnetic nanoparticles' shape and the resulting structural exhibits in the context of Hen Egg White Lysozyme (HEWL) [237]. The investigation yielded fascinating results, revealing the formation of dendritic structures with a unique microflower morphology. Researchers have delved into the incorporation of different moieties into dendrimers to discern their impact on the cationic surface charge, consequently influencing their interactions with proteins. A noteworthy example is the study focusing on two 4th generation cationic unmodified poly-(propylen-imine) dendrimers (PPI G4) and one anionic polyamidoamine dendrimer of the 3.5th generation (PAMAM G3.5), upon interaction with Hen Egg White Lysozyme (HEWL) [237]. Remarkably, the introduction of partially and fully coated maltose residues onto PPI dendrimers led to the transformation of their surface charge from cationic to neutral, forming PPI G4 dendrimers with Maltose Dense Shells (PPI-DS). These preformed PPI-DS dendrimers exhibited weaker interactions with HEWL, showcasing reduced dendrimer toxicity, and no adverse effects on the thermal stability of the lysozyme structure. However, both PPI-DS and PAMAM dendrimers were found to maintain strong interactions with lysozyme, attributed to the presence of both negative and positive charge clusters on the protein surface [238].

The anti-amyloidogenic properties of trehalose, tyrosine, and gallate were investigated using Hyperbranched Polyglycerol (HPG)--based multivalent dendrimers. The research revealed intriguing results, highlighting the effectiveness of these dendrimers in inhibiting the aggregation of Hen Egg White Lysozyme (HEWL). Among the tested dendrimers, the Trehalose-terminated dendrimers emerged as the most potent in preventing HEWL aggregation. The enhanced efficacy of Trehalose-terminated dendrimers was attributed to the presence of quinone-like structures, facilitating covalent binding with lysozyme aggregates. In contrast, the gallate-terminated dendrimer exhibited comparatively lower effectiveness. These functional terminal groups on the dendrimers play a pivotal role in their anti-amyloidogenic properties, offering multivalent binding with amyloid proteins owing to their small size. Moreover, the functional groups also contribute in preventing self-association and facilitating improved interactions of the nanoscale dendrimers with HEWL [239]. This remarkable research sheds light on the potential of functionalized dendrimers as promising candidates in the fight against protein aggregation-related diseases, opening new possibilities for advanced therapeutic strategies. Dendrimers encounter limitations, with challenges in achieving extensive production due to the synthesis processes, rendering them less cost-effective for specific applications. Concerns about potential toxicity, particularly evident in higher-generation dendrimers, may constrain their utility in biomedical applications. Attaining precise control over dendrimers' size and surface functionality proves challenging. Moreover, the tricky interactions between dendrimers and biological systems, coupled with potential immunogenicity, necessitate careful consideration in medical and therapeutic applications.

Lipids are hydrophobic, biomolecules characterized by their insolubility in water with functions ranging from energy storage and insulation to cell membrane composition and signaling. In the emerging “lipid-chaperon hypothesis,” self-assembled phospholipids interact with intrinsically disordered proteins, a process influenced by the critical micelles concentration (CMC) of free lipids in the aqueous phase. Two distinct phenomena are reported: i) when CMC is low, oxidized lipids in the aqueous phase act as chaperones, promoting the formation of lipid-protein complexes that either embed themselves into the membrane or cross the membrane, leading to fibrillogenesis; ii) in the presence of high CMC, the membrane undergoes pore-like formation, preventing the permeation of proteins, as lipid-protein complexation does not occur due to the unavailability of free lipids. If the intrinsically disordered protein is non-amyloidogenic, the lipid-protein complex never forms [240]. This hypothesis was demonstrated with gangliosides, abundant in neuronal membranes, forming stable complexes with amyloid-β isoforms [241]. This membrane-facilitated aggregation begins with the binding to α-helical intermediates and later transitions to form β-sheet rich amyloid fibers [231]. In physiological conditions, this method of traversing lipid bilayer membranes is speculated to be the mechanism by which amyloids exert their toxicity in biological entities [242].

Nanodiscs are discoidal lipid bilayer nanosystems surrounded by a membrane scaffold protein, designed for the solubilization and structural investigation of membrane proteins. Certain Polymethacrylate copolymers that are conjugated with nanodiscs and certain peptides forming nanodisc architectures [243–245] have been documented as regulators of amyloid aggregation kinetics, and further research would offer deeper insights into their interactions with lysozyme amyloids.

Certain compounds, recognized as active facilitators in the aggregation process, play a distinct role characterized by their notable influence on fibril formation. These compounds are linked to the pronounced elongation of fibrils, reduction in nucleation time, and enhanced aggregation propensity. Notably, this aggregation favoring effect is particularly pronounced under conditions of oxidative stress, which tends to favor protein unfolding. Acetylated-Lysine, hypobromous acid, anionic surfactants [Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), sodium bisethyl sulfosuccinate (AOT)], Cationic surfactants [cetyl trimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), dodecyl trimethylammonium bromide (DTAB)], SDS, vesicles formed of phosphatidylserine and phosphatidylcholine, Glyoxal, Silicone Oil, Phosphotidic Acid, Kappa-carrageenan were recognized as promoters of the aggregation process marked by distinct longer fibril formation and reduced nucleation time. Under conditions of oxidative stress, proteins favor unfolding and have a particular affinity for binding to the hydrophobic cores and executing charge neutralization where these compounds assist in their aggregation by inducing the formation of toxic conformations [96,158,246–252]. Their facilitative role is observed due to their increased affinity for binding to hydrophobic regions within proteins, coupled with their ability to neutralize charges. This phenomenon further favors the aggregation process by promoting the formation of secondary β-sheet structures. Consequently, it fosters the development of higher-ordered tertiary structures and encourages the self-assembly of more toxic aggregates, amplifying their potential for inducing adverse biological effects.

The process of protein aggregation, including lysozyme, is highly complex and arises from multiple misfolding events, leading to a series of transformations that ultimately result in the formation of oligomers, protofibrils, and resilient fibrils. Recent advancements in the field have revealed the remarkable potential of diverse small molecules, nanoparticles, functionalized materials, and bioactive peptides in inhibiting the in vitro production of amyloid fibrils across various protein targets, including Aβ peptide, α-synuclein, insulin, prion protein (PrP) amyloids, and lysozyme. Notably, lysozyme amyloidosis (ALys), a rare autosomal dominant hereditary systemic amyloidosis, is driven by the aggregation of lysozyme, leading to the formation of amyloid fibrils. This complex process manifests in a wide range of clinical and pathophysiological complications, affecting crucial organs and tissues such as the digestive tract, liver, spleen, kidneys, lymph nodes, skin, lachrymal, and salivary glands. Moreover ALys present a significant medical challenge due to its diverse and multi-organ involvement, highlighting the pressing need for further research and understanding to develop effective therapeutic approaches for this condition. The inhibitors identified in recent research reports have demonstrated a profound impact on the amyloidogenic process through distinct interaction modalities: binding to monomers to prevent oligomerization, engaging with oligomers to inhibit fibrillation, and intervening with amyloid fibrils to disassemble preformed fibrillar structures [184,253,254]. These discoveries not only open new vistas for scientific exploration but also offer promising avenues for therapeutic intervention, with the potential to address amyloid-related diseases with precision and efficacy. The quest for unraveling the underlying mechanisms and developing targeted interventions holds great promise in advancing our ability to combat amyloid-associated disorders effectively.
